The shrine buildings of Mt. Hachioji and Sanno 21 Shrines are depicted. ]]'''Hiyoshi Taisha'' is located in {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|qid=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Otsu City|Q202907|qid=Q202907|1=Ōtsu}}{{ill|lt=Sakamoto (Otsu City)|Q11425171|qid=Q11425171|lt=Sakamoto|1=Sakamoto}}. Also known as ``Hiyoshisha''. It was the {{ill|lt=guardian shrine #guardian shrine|Q97479976|qid=Q97479976|lt=guardian shrine|1=Chinjugami}} of {{ill|lt=Mt. Hiei|Q748040|qid=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}{{ill|lt=Enryakuji|Q917195|qid=Q917195|1=Enryaku-ji}}, the head temple of the {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|qid=Q668188|1=Tiantai}} Sanmon school in Japan. The name in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' Shinto Name Book is ``Hiyoshi Shrine'', but after the medieval period it was called ``Sanno Daigongen'', {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sanno Gongen|1=UNKNOWN}}, Hiyoshi Sanno Gongen, Hiyoshi Sannosha''' etc.{{Sfn|Okada|2003|p=5}}{{Sfn|Okada|2003|p=21}}. ===Summary=== It is the head office of approximately 3,800 companies nationwide, {{ill|lt=Hiyoshi Shrine|Q11505065|qid=Q11505065|lt=Hiyoshi, Hie, and Sanno Shrines|1=Hiyoshi Shrine}}. The name in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' Shinto Name Book is ``Hiyoshi Shrine'', but after the medieval period it was called ``Sanno Daigongen'', {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sanno Gongen|1=UNKNOWN}}, Hiyoshi Sanno Gongen, Hiyoshi Sannosha''' etc.{{Sfn|Okada|2003|p=5}}{{Sfn|Okada|2003|p=21}}. {{ill|lt=Japanese macaque|Q191781|qid=Q191781|lt=monkey|1=Japanese macaque}} is a divine messenger and is called a divine monkey (masaru). The precincts of 400,000 {{ill|lt=square meters|Q25343|qid=Q25343|1=Square metre}} centered on '''Nishi Hongu (Omiya)'' and '''Higashi Hongu (Ninomiya)'' are designated as a national {{ill|lt=historic site|Q1081138|qid=Q1081138|1=Historic site}}. The company name "Hiyoshi" used to be pronounced "hie," but after {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|qid=Q362|1=World War II}} the official reading was "hiyoshi."The pronunciation of "hiyoshi" is as follows. * [http://hiyoshitaisha.jp/faq/ Hiyoshi Taisha official website "Frequently asked questions"] (accessed May 6, 2012)* [http://www.rirc.or.jp/xoops/modules/xxxxx05/detail.php?id=246 Hiyoshi Taisha section of the International Research Institute of Religions] (accessed November 6, 2016). {{ill|lt=Torii|Q234731|qid=Q234731|1=Torii}} has a mountain shape at the top indicating {{ill|lt=Mt. Hiei|Q748040|qid=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}{{ill|lt=Enryakuji|Q917195|qid=Q917195|1=Enryaku-ji}}. It is called the ``Sanno torii'' (gassho torii, general torii) due to its unique form. {{ISBN2 | 4-582-94496-5}}.In "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" (712), it is written that {{ill|lt=Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105|qid=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} resides in {{ill|lt=Iwaza|Q11587880|qid=Q11587880|lt=Iwa|1=Iwakura rock}} near the peak of Mt. Hachioji at the eastern foot of Mt. Hiei, and this is the oldest record. name="Tourism Agency"/>. However, Hiyoshi-sha was founded before the Kojiki, and it is difficult to know exactly about its origins {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|p=1}}. The modern Hiyoshi Taisha homepage and pamphlets say, ``Approximately 2100 years ago, in the year {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|qid=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}7 ('Nihon Shoki' says it was 91 BC, but academically it dates back to the 3rd or 4th century {{efn|Emperor Sujin's reign was in '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}' 97 BC). 30 B.C., but he is thought to have lived in the middle of the early third century. It was founded at the Kyoto Imperial Court Culture Research Institute|accessdate=2025-07-20}}. name="Pamphlet"/>. In addition, in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hiyoshishanegiguchidensho|1=UNKNOWN}}'', it says, ``In the year of Emperor Sujin, {{ill|lt=Edict|Q11631301|qid=Q11631301|1=imperial edict}} (Mikotonori) and the god Oyama Kui, who sits on the Amanami no Tsuka on Mt. Ushio (Mt. Hachioji) Mitama) is enshrined at Yamamoto (the foot of the mountain)," but this book is known to be a forgery created at the end of the Edo period and during the Meiji Restoration.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}{{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=1 90}}{{Sfn|Take|2020|p=191}}{{efn|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shigekuni Shigekuni|1=UNKNOWN}}, who is thought to be the creator of "Hiyoshishanegiguchidensho," was born in the seventh year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|qid=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}}. The enshrined deity of Ninomiya is Oyama Kuiwakon based on the edict, but {{ill|lt=John Breen|Q6223046|qid=Q6223046|1=John Breen (scholar)}} points out that the edict does not exist.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=160}}. Oyama Kuijin has been the main character of the Sanno Festival since the Meiji period {{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=148-149}}. }}. According to tradition, Ohibie (Sanno Gongen, Omiya Gongen), the deity enshrined at Omiya (now Nishimoto Shrine) in the west shrine, is the Miwa God of Yamato ({{ill|lt=Omiya Shrine|Q7 05542|qid=Q7 05542|lt=Miwa Myojin}}), that is, {{ill|qid=Q276944|lt=Okuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Okumi no Kami|1=Ōkuninushi}}, is the enshrined deity of Ninomiya (currently Higashihongu) in the eastern shrine building. Kohiei (Ofe) (Ninomiya Gongen) is the god of {{ill|lt=Hachioji Gongen|Q11391615|qid=Q11391615|lt=Mt. Hachioji|1=Hachiōji Gongen}} at the eastern foot of Mt. Hiei, and the local Hiei deity, Oyamakui. {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}{{Sfn|Suzuki|2008|pp=50-52}} who is said to be a god ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamasueno Daisushikami|1=UNKNOWN}}). The modern Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine holds the theory that the three-wheeled deity Tenchicho-kansho has been adopted for the shrine, and that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenchi|Q314802|qid=Q314802|1=Emperor Tenji}} moved the capital from Yamato to Otsu, he welcomed the god of Mt. Miwa in Yamato and enshrined it as the deity of the {{ill|lt=protected state|Q11651185|qid=Q11651185|1=chingo kokka}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://hiyoshitaisha.jp/images/pdf/hiyoshimitsuori.pdf|title=Sanno Sohongu Hiyoshi Taisha|website=|publisher=Hiyoshi Taisha|accessdate=2025-07-20}}. Furthermore, there is no reliable historical evidence that the Miwagami was solicited to Omiya, and it has been pointed out that this theory was likely created in later generations {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}. Omiya and Ninomiya were originally the gods of Mt. Hiei, ``Hie no kami,'' and there is also a view that they are the gods of the large and small peaks of Mt. Hiei, and that they are twin deities.{{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}{{Sfn|Suzuki|2008|p=70}}Miwa-kami, the deity enshrined in Omiya, was the guardian deity of the dynasty, and because it was consistent with {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|qid=Q668188|1=Tiantai}}, which preaches the protection of the nation, it is believed to be the original landowner deity. Ninomiya was deprived of his position, and Omiya was given higher rank and respect. {{ill|lt=Sanno faith|Q11470037|qid=Q11470037|1=Sannō Faith}} has its origins in the {{ill|lt=mountain faith|Q93697439|qid=Q93697439|1=Mountain worship}} of Mt. Hiei, but various beliefs have been compounded throughout history, and it is also characterized by the {{ill|lt=syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|Q2279088|qid=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, with two books in the East and the West. With the shrine as its center, there were 21 Sanno shrines, including the Kamishichi Shrine (Sanno Seven Shrines), and 108 subsidiary shrines within and outside the precincts, forming one large sacred site, and its beliefs were complex. The Sanno faith is filled with many gods, but Kamishichisha forms the core {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=271}}. In the medieval period, the {{ill|lt=ten Zen masters|Q123524325|qid=Q123524325|1=Jūzenji}} of Kamishichisha were {{ill|lt=possessed|Q1782082|qid=Q1782082|1=Spirit possession}} by {{ill|lt=shaman|Q3687892|qid=Q3687892|lt=shaman|1=Wu (shaman)}} and gave {{jal to the people. Ink|Oracle}} Known as a wild childish god of a sacred mountain, he was especially feared and worshiped as a god of miraculous powers.Sanno Sansho (Omiya, Ninomiya, Seimako [current The shrine building of Usanomiya]) was built by the 4th year of Ninna (888) in the early Heian period, and the Sanno Shichisha was built by the 3rd year of Eiyo (1115). ) is thought to have been formed by {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|p=147}}{{Sfn|Sugawara|1987|pp=4-5}}. Prior to the Meiji period {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|qid=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, the faith of Hiyoshi Shrine developed together with Enryaku-ji Temple, was worshiped fervently by the monks of Enryaku-ji Temple, and boasted its prestige as its strongest bearer {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=271}}. The {{ill|lt=Sanno faith|Q11470037|qid=Q11470037|1=Sannō Faith}} of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu syncretism|Q2279088|qid=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, centered on Hiyoshi Shrine, gained a strong faith as a god of protection of Buddhism, royal law, and protection of the nation, and as the Tendai sect spread nationwide. He was worshiped in various places and became a guardian deity of the area. It flourished as the {{ill|lt=guardian deity|Q97479976|qid=Q97479976|1=Chinjugami}} shrine of Mt. Hiei Enryakuji Temple, the head temple of the Sanmon school of the Tendai sect. In the latter half of the 9th century, Enryaku-ji Temple increasingly interfered with the rituals of Hiyoshi Shrine, and the Hiyoshi Shrine chief {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ikube|1=UNKNOWN}}, in an attempt to compete with Enryaku-ji Temple, changed the enshrined deity of Ninomiya from Oyama Kui no kami to '{{jalink|Japan It is thought that he changed his name to {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=111-112}}, the first god after the creation of heaven and earth in the Shoki |Q813935}}.Although it was in decline at the end of the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|qid=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, Enryaku-ji Temple came into conflict with {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|qid=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}, and Nobunaga burned down Mt. (1571)|Q10748887|Hiei Shrine was destroyed by fire. After a 10-year hiatus, the shrine was restored after a 10-year hiatus under the leadership of Yukimaru Shogenji (Yukimaru Yukibe, 1512 - 1592), a member of the Iwaibe clan who had escaped and was the general secretary of Hiyoshi Shrine.{{Sfn|Sagai|1992|p=31}}. Many of the existing shrine buildings of Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine were built between 1586 and 1596, at the height of the {{ill|lt=Momoyama Culture|Q11131431|qid=Q11131431|1=Momoyama Culture}} period. Buildings from that time still exist in Kyoto, but it is rare to find so many of them crowded together, making it a valuable sight.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://shinsho-plus.shueisha.co.jp/column/nippon_junrei/6619/2|author={{ill|lt=Alex Carr|Q4717298|qid=Q4717298|1=Alex Kerr (Japanologist)}}|date=2019-8-15|title=Pilgrimage to Japan Kotoba serialized version ① Hiyoshi Taisha, Jigendo, Ishiyamadera (Shiga Prefecture) Momoyama Roof | website=Shueisha Shinsho Plus | publisher=Shueisha | accessdate=2025-07-31}}.In the latter half of the 17th century, Hiyoshi Shrine was losing not only its ritual rights but also its management rights to Enryaku-ji Temple, and also had animosity toward {{ill|lt=Sanno Shinto#Sanno Kazumi Shinto|Q4407806|qid=Q4407806|lt=Sanno Kazumi Shinto|1=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}}, which enshrined Tokugawa Ieyasu as {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu#Ieyasu's deification|Q171977|qid=Q171977|lt=Tosho Daigongen|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. The priests attempted to resist Enryaku-ji Temple, smuggled out the objects of worship and Buddhist utensils from the seven shrines and burned them, insisted on abolishing the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism at Hiyoshi Shrine, and refused to allow monks to enter the precincts. Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=88-91}}{{Sfn|Murayama|1973|p=499}}{{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=89}}{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://shinsho-plus.shueisha.co.jp/column/nippon_junrei/6619/2|author={{ill|lt=Alex Carr|Q4717298|qid=Q4717298|1=Alex Kerr (Japanologist)}}|date=2019-8-15|title=Pilgrimage to Japan Kotoba serialized version ① Hiyoshi Taisha, Jigendo, Ishiyamadera (Shiga Prefecture) Sanno Torii|website=Shueisha Shinsho Plus|publisher=Shueisha|accessdate=2025-07-31}}. This incident was investigated in Edo, the shrine leaders were punished, and the Buddhist statues were restored. name="car"/>. The Hiyoshi Shrine family lost all authority except to serve the festival, and they accumulated anger, dissatisfaction, and antipathy toward the rule of Enryakuji Temple, which led to the intense Meiji period. } is thought to have led to {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=88-91}}{{Sfn|Murayama|1973|p=499}}{{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=89}}.Under the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|qid=Q1970535|lt=Ordinance for the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (1868), harsh {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|1=UNKNOWN}}, and this uproar is widely known. name="Tourism Agency"/>. The priests burned more than 1,000 Buddhist-related artifacts (some thousands) at Hiyoshi Shrine. |title=Hiyoshi Taisha |website=Multilingual commentary database of local tourism resources|publisher={{ill|lt=Tourism Agency|Q6157871|qid=Q6157871|1=Japan Tourism Agency}}|accessdate=2025-06-18}}. In addition, in response to the order for the separation of Shinto and Buddhism, the Buddhist name of the shrine, Hiyoshi Daigongen, was changed to ``Hiyoshi Shrine'' {{Sfn|Keishuro|2007|p=56}}. Sanno worship centered on Hiyoshi Shrine is a syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, and its separation from Buddhism drastically changed the gods and their hierarchy at Hiyoshi Shrine . The current Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine was reorganized in the Meiji period of the late 19th century, including its layout, the main deity and its hierarchy, and ceremonies such as the Sanno Festival. The main deities enshrined were replaced.{{efn|The Japan Tourism Agency says that four of the seven main deities of Sanno Shichisha Shrine were replaced, and John Breen says that six deities other than Okimi no Kami were replaced{{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=148-149}}. }}, a new deity system unrelated to Buddhism was formed. Along with this reorganization of the gods, the Sanno Festival has also been restructured . Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine has a history of complicated religious conflicts. The current Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine has a pure Shinto style, including the deity enshrined, the rituals, the people involved in the rituals, and the decorations and utensils inside the shrine, and the rituals of the pre-modern Hiyoshi Shrine, in which the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism was an essential element, have now been lost {{Sfn|Okada|2003|pp=10-11}}.{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) was one of {{ill|lt=22 shrines|Q215320|qid=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (Shimohachisha). The old shrine rank in {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System#Government Shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and currently it is {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shrine Honcho|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. According to Hiyoshi Taisha, after the war, due to the abolition of the shrine rank system, there was no longer a ``{{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system#government shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}'', so in order to prevent confusion with regional branch shrines, and because it is the main shrine, the word ``jinja'' was changed to ``taisha,'' and the precept was also changed to the more familiar ``hiyoshi,'' making it ``Hiyoshi Taisha.''{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://hiyoshitaisha.jp/faq/|date=2019-8-15|title=Frequently Asked Questions|website=Sanno Sohonzan Hiyoshi Taisha|publisher=|accessdate=2025-07-31}}. {{ill|lt=Precincts|Q11429002|qid=Q11429002|1=keidai}} Omiya Bridge has been selected as one of {{ill|lt=Japan's 100 Famous Bridges|Q11508656|qid=Q11508656|1=100 Fine Bridges of Japan}}. === Auspicious origin === {{main|Sanno Faith}} === Higashihongu (Ninomiya) === The enshrined deity is called Kohiei-kami (Obie-myojin, Ninomiya Gongen), and according to tradition, it is said to be the local Hiei-kami, ``the god of Hie'' {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}{{Sfn|Suzuki|2008|pp=50-52}}. Kohiei is the god of Mt. Hachioji (Mt. Kohiei) at the eastern foot of Mt. Hiei, and is said to be {{ill|lt=Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105|qid=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} (also known as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamasueno Daishujin|1=UNKNOWN}}). {{Sfn|Okada|2003|p=20}} is the {{ill|lt=god of the mountains|Q839254|qid=Q839254|1=Yama-no-Kami}} of the southern Omi Basin and has the personality of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=god of agriculture|1=UNKNOWN}}. He is the god of Mt. Hiei, and is said to be a mountain god and landowner god. {{Sfn|Okada|2003|p=8}}. He is also said to be the Tokodateson of the country or the mountain king in "Nihonshoki" {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=275}}. In "Hiyoshisha Negiguchidensho", Hiyoshisha is said to have been moved from the top of Mt. Hie to its current location in {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|qid=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}} in the year 7.(In addition, this historical material is considered to be {{ill|lt=false manuscript|Q42757690|qid=Q42757690|1=forged document}}{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}{{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}).In the literature, "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" says, "Oyamakui no Kami, his name is Yamasue no Daishukami. This god sits on the mountain of Hie in {{ill|lt=Ōmi Province|Q843882|qid=Q843882|lt=Kindankai Province|1=Ōmi Province}}," and this is the first mention of Oyamakui no Kami.[http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=23 Hiyoshi Taisha] (Shiga Prefectural Shrine Agency). Hie no Yama is the later name of {{ill|lt=Mt. Hiei|Q748040|qid=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}. From the description in the Kojiki, it is known that he was one of the 16 gods born between {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōtoshi|1=UNKNOWN}} and Inohime, the daughter of the Shinkatsuo Subijin, and was considered to be the same deity as {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|qid=Q710302|lt=Matsuojin|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} in Kazuno District, Yamashiro Province.{{Sfn|Miyaie|2007|p=107}}The oldest and most comprehensive story about the origins of Ninomiya is the one recorded in the ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotenki|1=UNKNOWN}}'' compiled in the Kamakura period, and this is the ``Sanno Engi,'' which was passed down to the Ikube clan of the Hiyoshi Shrine family. It is thought that this was established together with ``Sannoji'' (the doctrine of {{ill|lt=Sanno Shinto|Q4407806|qid=Q4407806|1=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} of Tendai Shinto) written by the learned monk of Enryaku-ji Temple {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=272}}. In ``Kotenki'', there are two theories that differ in content and character: ``the theory that Ninomiya = landowner deity'' and ``the theory that Sanno's three saints descended at the same time'' {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=272}}. “Ninomiya = Concerning the ``Landlord God Theory,'' in ``Kotenki,'' one of the Seven Buddhas of the past (the seven Buddhas who appeared before Shakyamuni Buddha), when he was the grandson of a detained Buddha, rode the waves of the South Sea and chanted the phrase ``All beings are Buddhas'' in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nirvana Sutra|1=UNKNOWN}}, and when the waves stopped, It is said that he traveled to Japan to spread Buddhism in the land, and was washed up in the cedar cave of Kohiei on Samukase-dake, where he was enshrined. According to {{ill|lt=Hiroko Yamamoto|Q11468773|qid=Q11468773|1=Hiroko Yamamoto}}, Kohiei is not located within the shrine grounds of Hiyoshi Shrine, where Ninomiya is enshrined, but in the mountains near Shuzenmine, which belongs to the west tower of Mt. Hiei, and ``Samukasedake'' also exists near here {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=272-273}}. Also, in "Kotenki", Ninomiya is said to be the "landlord of Japan" {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=273}}.The theory of the simultaneous advent of the Sanno and the Three Saints in "Kotenki" was written by {{ill|lt=Chōken|Q11565801|qid=Q11565801|1=Chōken}}, a learned monk of Enryaku-ji Temple who was a master of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sermons|1=UNKNOWN}}. ``The Sanno Three Saints descended from heaven at the same time.Therefore, it would be questionable to single out Ninomiya as the Jishu Myojin.''{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=274}}. The main shrine of Seimako was built by {{ill|lt=Sōō (monk)|Q11582028|qid=Q11582028|lt=Sōō|1=Sōō}} (831- It was established as an independent building during the renovation of Omiya in the period of 918, and it is said that this was the occasion when the "Three Saints" of Omiya, Ninomiya, and Seimako were established. It formed its own name and oral tradition through its association with Tendai sect teachings such as ``Isshin Sankan'' and ``Sanshin Sokuichi'' {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=274}}. The origin of Ninomiya evolved through the competition and coexistence of those that talk about Ninomiya alone and those that talk about the trio of gods called ``Sansho'' {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=274}}. In "Kotenki," Saicho solicited Sansho under the cedar tree in Kohiei, {{ill|lt=receives the precepts|Q5367041|qid=Q5367041|1=Buddhist initiation}} (ordained priesthood), and Sansho is said to have become a monk.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=282-283}}.In ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamake Yokoki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', two types of auspicious origin are proposed: ``Kohieijin = Kunijotateson'' theory and ``Kohieijin = Yamano'' theory {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=275}}. This is the work of a writer who specialized in recording on Mt. Hiei in the Middle Ages, and who formed and handed down the oral tradition of Dharma.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=275-276}}. “Kohieijin = According to the ``Kunijotachison'' theory, set in the Genesis story of the creation of heaven and earth in the Nihon Shoki, the first god to appear, Kunijotachison, descends from the sky for the first time. This high peak is Kohiei, and Kunitachi no Mikoto is said to be the same as Kohiei (Ninomiya), and is considered to be the landowner deity of Japan {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=275-276}}. In addition to Ninomiya, according to the ``Jingi Senryo'' theory, all the gods of Kamishichisha are syncretized with the gods of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kiki Mythology|1=UNKNOWN}}, and for example, they were made into priest-shaped or child-shaped gods.{{ill|lt=The Ten Zen Masters|Q123524325|qid=Q123524325|1=Jūzenji}} are considered to be the child god {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|qid=Q1056017|lt=Qiong Qiongzhong|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=276}}. “Kohieijin = The "Sanno" theory is linked to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=national birth myth|1=UNKNOWN}} by {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|qid=Q813858|1=Izanagi}} and {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|qid=Q682306|1=Izanami}}, and the theory that Japan's land was created by two gods The ``first land'' when the mountain was created is said to be ``Mt. Kohiei'', and Ninomiya is therefore ``the landowner of our country'' and is given the title ``Mountain King'' as the mountain king of Mt. Hiei.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=278}}.A strange theory that inherits the theory of the coming of Kohiei Ninomiya (Ninomiya = theory of landowner deity) in "Kotenki" is the genesis story surrounding Mt. Kohiei (mother mountain) in "Fuso Kogo Reiisoshu," which is the third part of the Hiyoshi Sanno Reioki in "Yamake Yoryoki," "Gonjin Reiosho." {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=289}}. It is said that Ohara's {{ill|lt=Kenshin|Q11665126|qid=Q11665126|1=Kenshin}} (1130 - 1192), the founder of the kiki family, made him a secretary who did not allow inheritance without permission, but the actual author is presumed to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshigen|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Kamakura period, who is said to be the compiler of ``Yamake Yoryoki.''{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=289}}. The story is said to have been written down by Torakura about a white-bearded old man who has lived since the beginning of the world.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=290}}. ``The energy of ``{{ill|lt=Taiji|Q53685|qid=Q53685|1=Taiji (philosophy)}}'' still has no form, and the heavens and the earth are in a state of chaos.'' In this chaotic ocean, waves of five colors arise, echoing ``All sentient beings have Buddha nature, the Tathagata is eternally inhabited and changeable,'' and ``three rings of golden light'' appear and float away, creating the heavens, the earth, and the directions of the world. When order and law were given to the world, the ``Three Rings of Golden Light'' became the ``Three Divine Lights,'' or Sanno Sansei, and went to the {{ill|lt=Ashihara Chugoku|Q1096244|qid=Q1096244|1=Ashihara no Nakatsukuni}} (earthly world) to spread Buddhism.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=291-293}}. Next, the origin of Ninomiya is talked about, and it is said that the primordial five-colored waves crashed on the Kohiei coast, and that the pure waves condensed upwards and formed mountains, while the murky waves stagnated downwards, forming valleys and the earth (an appropriation of the Chinese theory of the creation of the heavens and the earth) {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=291-294}}. Kohiei is said to have originally been a sea, and this is also seen in ``Shokoku Miki Sei Monogatari'' and ``Hiyoshisanno Riseiki.''{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=291-293}}{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=302}}. Kohiei, which was born from the primordial five-colored waves, is the sacred mountain that founded the world, and is said to be called ``Mother Wave Mountain,'' meaning ``mother mountain of waves,'' and it is presumed that this is the book of Genesis that is alluded to as ``an important secret decision'' in ``Yamake Yoryoki.''{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=294-295}}. At the foot of Kohiei, Kohiei witnessed the sacrament in which heavenly and terrestrial phenomena were created (from the opposing forces of yin and yang, (Easy)|Q11420141|Four elephants}} are created, and from the four elephants, {{ill|lt=Eight trigrams|Q340846|qid=Q340846|1=Bagua}} is determined, and by stacking Eight Trigrams on Eight Trigrams, sixty-four trigrams are determined. When heaven and earth were established and human affairs were determined, the earth god sat in the center of the eight trigrams and ruled over ``earth'', embodying ``earth.'' In other words, Kohiei was enshrined at the foot of Kohiei, but at the same time he was the earth god who sat at the center of the world {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=294-296}}. Ninomiya is said to be the god that governs the creation of the world, and Mt. Kohiei (Mt. Mother Wave) is said to be the most fundamental place, and the divine name Ninomiya reflects the motive behind the creation of the universe, and is explained as ``Kenkon no Ryōgu, yin and yang, enveloping both qi.'' {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=296-297}}. === Nishimotogu Shrine (Omiya) === Since the Middle Ages, there have been two main traditions. nk|Sanno Gongen|Q11470054}}, Omiya Gongen) was the emperor in 668 when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|qid=Q314802|1=Emperor Tenji}} ascended the throne. It is said that the three-wheeled deity of Yamato Province ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miwa Myojin|1=UNKNOWN}}, the guardian deity of the family and imperial castle), or {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Okomi no Kami|1=Ōkuninushi}}, was solicited. The other theory is that {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}} was solicited by {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|qid=Q449943|1=Saichō}}, the founder of Japan's {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|qid=Q668188|1=Tiantai}}. === Research === ;The beginning of faith The unique faith is thought to have originated from the belief that the conical {{ill|lt=Hachioji Gongen|Q11391615|qid=Q11391615|lt=Mt. Hachioji|1=Hachiōji Gongen}} (also known as Mt. Kohiei, Mt. Hie, Mt. Namimo, and Mt. Ushio), which rises to the west of Sakamoto town with the Ohiei mountains in the background, is a sacred mountain.{{Sfn|Suzuki|2008|pp=49-50}}. There is a sacred spring (well) in the lower hall of {{ill|lt=Juzenshi|Q123524325|qid=Q123524325|1=Jūzenji}} Shrine (currently Jishikagu Shrine), which was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gonnegi|1=UNKNOWN}} of Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine.{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sagai Ken|1=UNKNOWN}} estimates that a place for rituals developed around this sacred place at the foot of the mountain overlooking Mt. Hachioji, where spiritual springs gush out.{{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=157-159}}. Ken Sagai says that the first form of worship in this area, before Shrine Shinto, was that ``the great rock near the summit was the place where a god descended or was imprisoned {{ill|lt=Iwaza|Q11587880|qid=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}}; It is believed that daily rituals were held throughout the year at Juzenshisha Shrine at the foot of the mountain, where spiritual springs gush out, and rituals were held at Iwaza on the mountaintop twice in spring and autumn.{{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=157-159}} Hachioji and Sannomiya are both said to have appeared in Kindaigan, and are gods with Kindaigan as the Iwaza seat, and are thought to originate from Iwaza rituals.At the eastern foot of Mt. Hachioji (east mountain king), there are a large number of round tombs (Hiyoshi kofun group) from the late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}} period, which are thought to have been the object of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ancestral spirit worship|1=UNKNOWN}} {{Sfn|Suzuki|2008|p=70}}. Ninomiya is thought to have originally been located near the Odari River, deeper than the current shrine grounds, and is currently located in an area where a group of ancient tombs is concentrated, on top of which the land has been leveled. According to Ken Sagai, Ninomiya is a distant sacred place where you can worship Mt. Hachioji, where the god Oyamakui is seated, and the shrine at Ninomiya is a shrine where the god at the top of the mountain temporarily descends to the foot of the mountain. It is believed that as rituals became a regular occurrence, it became the main shrine where divine spirits resided.{{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=152-153}} ;Ninomiya = landowner theory Hiroko Yamamoto argues that the ``Ninomiya = landowner god theory'' of Ninomiya-enki is based on the pilgrimage to Mt. (Mt. Hiei)|Q1330684|1000 Days of Mine-gyo}}), and records show that Kohiei, which is one point on the pilgrimage route from the west tower to Yokogawa, was talked about as an essential element, and he clarifies how this theory spread.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=272-273}}. ;Ohieijin = Sanno theoryThe theory of ``Kohiei-jin = Sanno'' in ``Yamake Yoryokki'' is based on the ancient Chinese views of Jingi, souls (demon gods), Jongmyo {{ill|lt=Shajik|Q5352684|qid=Q5352684|1=Soil and grain}}, and Gogyo-kan, such as in ``{{ill|lt=Gogyo Daigi|Q18863192|qid=Q18863192|1=五行大義}},'' and Omiya is ``Jongmyojin'' and Ninomiya is ``Shashin.'' Seimako is considered to be the ``kami no kami'' (chief of the five grains), and as the land is vast and cannot be worshiped everywhere, it is explained that the land is sealed off and enshrined at the shrine {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=278-281}}. Sanno Shinto, the Jingi theory by the Enryaku-ji scribes, relies heavily on the ``Gogyo Daigi,'' and there is an intersection between Gogyo thought, Shinto, and Buddhism {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=280-281}}. Miyaji (Miyaji, Miyaji Hoshi), a lower-class priest who served Hiyoshi Shrine {{efn|Miyaji, who were actually in charge of festivals and also managed the shrine building, are said to have their origin in the Three Holy Precepts by Saicho {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=282-283}}. }} was wearing a yellow robe, and yellow is the color that symbolizes earth in the Chinese theory of the five elements, and Ninomiya is combined with the Chinese legendary {{ill|lt=Yellow Emperor|Q29201|qid=Q29201|1=Yellow Emperor}}, and Hiroko Yamamoto , pointed out that Ninomiya has the aspect of ``the king of the earth who rules the center and the earth (yellow)'' based on Chinese thought, especially the five elements philosophy.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=282-283}} = The Sanno theory states that the topology of Kohiei and the landowner-god character of Ninomiya were immediately elevated to a mythical level through the Chinese philosophy of the five elements.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=289}}. In this way, Ninomiya Engi has strengthened its abstractness, primordiality, and universality, but along with this, the uniqueness of the location and its ties to the region have been weakened {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=289}}.;Kohieijin = Kokujotachi Sonsetsu “Kohiei-kami = According to the ``Kunijotachison'' theory, Ninomiya was expanded from the landowner deity of Mt. Hiei to the landowner deity of Japan, and the five-colored flowers that bloomed on the large cedar of Kohiei, where Ninomiya is enshrined, symbolize the east, west, north, south, and center ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=five elements|1=UNKNOWN}} It is a thought) that {{ill|lt=segmentation|Q2266173|qid=Q2266173|1=Speech segmentation}} of the born universe, and this great cedar is considered to be the primordial spiritual tree, the cosmic tree {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=275-276}}. ;Questions on the theory that “Omiya = Miwagami”Regarding the time when Miwagami was enshrined on Mt. Hiei, there are two theories since the medieval period: the {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenchi|Q314802|qid=Q314802|lt=Tenchi Dynasty|1=Emperor Tenji}} Kansho theory (668) and Japan's {{jalink|Tendai sect|Q6 There are two theories, the {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|qid=Q449943|1=Saichō}} Kansho theory, which is the ancestor of the 68188}}, but there are no reliable historical sources for either theory {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}. It has been revealed that ``Hiyoshisha Negiguchidensho'', which describes the Tenchi Dynasty's solicitation theory, is a forgery, and this theory cannot be confirmed before 1081, and the theory that it was solicited during the Saicho era has also been denied, and the solicitation of the Miwagami cannot be confirmed with reliable historical materials {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}. Furthermore, according to Yohei Ikeda of Kokugakuin University, the original Miwa deity at Hiyoshi Shrine was believed to be Sanno Sansho, not Ohiei no Kami (Omiya), and the theory that ``Sanno Sansho = Miwa God'' was created by combining ``Miwa'' and ``Sansho''.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://researchmap.jp/IkedaYohei/published_papers/32177345 |title=Land sovereignty and Taho Tathagata (overview) |website=researchmap|publisher=National Research and Development Agency Japan Science and Technology Agency|accessdate=2025-06-22}}. ; "Omiya/Ninomiya = God of Hieru" theoryKazuhiko Yoshida of Nagoya City University (Historian)|Q17210368|Kazuhiko Yoshida}} rejects the theory that Omiya is the Miwakami based on historical sources as something that was created in later generations, and argues that Omiya and Miwakami should be considered separately. Ninomiya was originally ``the god of Hie,'' which was the god of Mt. Hiei, and this evolved into two gods, Ohhiei and Kohiei in the late 9th century {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}. Hiromasa Suzuki of Teikyo University says that Omiya and Ninomiya are a pair of deities, with Mount Shimeidake on Mt. Hiei as the inner shrine and Mt. Hachioji as the mountain shrine {{Sfn|Suzuki|2008|p=70}}. ;Questions about the context in which Omiya and Ninomiya were enshrined Yohei Ikeda says, “Omiya = He doubts not only the theory that Ninomiya was the first deity to be enshrined and Omiya was enshrined later. It was later thought that Ohiei-kami, or Miwa-kami, was worshiped.'' However, it is pointed out that the word ``Jishu'' originally did not have the meaning of ``worshiped first,'' and that this was due to the teachings of Sanno Shinto . The theory that ``Sanno Sansei = Miwa-kami'' was established, and ``Furthermore, with the establishment of the current name of Jishujin (Kohie-kami), Omiya Gongen was considered to be the Miwa-kami that was solicited later.These are all popular theories, and the shrine family's Omiya Engi was established as a result of accepting these theories.'' ;When the shrine was establishedThe transition from Nature Shinto to Shrine Shinto was influenced by Buddhism, and Shrine Shinto can be said to be a Buddhist form {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=111-112}}. According to Ken Sagai, as the temples of Enryaku-ji Temple were being built one after another in Mt. Hiei, the Shintoism in this area gradually progressed to become Shinto shrines, including Ninomiya (Satomiya), Omiya, Seimako, Kyoujin, {{ill|lt=Hachioji Gongen|Q113916 15|qid=Q113916 15|lt=Hachioji}} (Yamamiya), Juzenji (Satomiya), and Sannomiya (Yamamiya) shrines were built in this order. {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|p=147}} According to Masato Sato, the shrine buildings of Sanno Sansei (Omiya, Ninomiya, and Seimako [now Usamiya]) were completed by 888 in the early Heian period (888). Since the founding date of Sannomiya is not certain, it is difficult to determine the upper limit of the date of establishment of the Sanno Seven Shrines, but the upper limit of the establishment date of the Sanno Seven Shrines is the fifth year of Manju (1029) and the lower limit is the third year of Eiji (1115), and even if we go even further back, there is no doubt that they were established in the sixth year of Yasunobu (1141) {{Sfn|Sugawara|1987|pp=4-5}}. === History === === Before Meiji === In {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|qid=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 7th year ({{ill|lt=788|Q30721|qid=Q30721|1=788}}), {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|qid=Q449943|1=Saichō}} built Hieizan-ji Temple (later {{jali nk|Enryaku-ji Temple|Q917195}}) Ichijōdekkan-in Temple (later {{ill|lt=Enryaku-ji Nemoto Chudo|Q15717263|qid=Q15717263|lt=Konpon Chudo|1=Konpon Chūdō}}) was built, and Hiyoshi Shrine, which enshrines the landowner deity of Mt. Hiei, was revered as a guardian deity. Then, with the transfer of the capital to {{ill|lt=Heiankyo|Q751907|qid=Q751907|1=Heian-kyō}} in the 13th year of Enryaku ({{ill|lt=794|Q30887|qid=Q30887|1=794}}), Hiyoshi-sha Shrine corresponded to the {{ill|lt=Kimon|Q11673539|qid=Q11673539|1=Kimon}} of Kyoto, so it came to be revered by the country as a shrine that protects demons and protects against disasters. In the 3rd year of Tenan (859), the god level was conferred on Kohiei.{{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|pp=190-191}}. In the latter half of the 9th century, Enryaku-ji Temple increasingly interfered with the rituals of Hiyoshi Shrine, and the Hiyoshi Shrine chief {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ikube|1=UNKNOWN}}, in an attempt to compete with Enryaku-ji Temple, changed the enshrined deity of Ninomiya from Oyama Kui no kami to '{{jalink|Japan It is thought that he changed his name to {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=111-112}}, the first god after the creation of heaven and earth in the Shoki |Q813935}}.In 887, the Tendai priest {{ill|lt=Enchin|Q1972505|qid=Q1972505|1=Enchin}}, who was the head priest of Enryaku-ji Temple, sent two people to Enryaku-ji Temple for the deities of Ohiei and Kohiei. {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|pp=190-191}} to the Imperial Court to obtain the annual quota of {{ill|lt=monastics|Q1074258|qid=Q1074258|1=Pabbajjā}} ({{ill|lt=obudo|Q11488740|qid=Q11488740|1=obudo}}) people assigned to each sect. The description of the two gods being together can be seen for the first time in the above text {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|pp=190-191}}. Furthermore, Ōhiei and Kohiei are the names of peaks (mountains) and have been in use since before Enchin, and Ōhiei and Kohiei are the names of the gods of those peaks {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|pp=190-191}}. Kazuhiko Yoshida believes that it was around this time that the deity of Mt. Hiei, ``Hie no kami,'' developed into two deities, and developed the names of the large and small peaks into the twin deities of Ohiei and Kohiei. It is estimated that Enchin is the one who is responsible for this, and that his purpose is to secure two Nenbundo people by making them two gods.{{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|pp=190-192}}. Also, around the time of Enchin, the Sanno Sanno deities of Ohiei, Kohiei, and Seomashi Myojin were established {{Sfn|Bu|2020|p=196}}.As Enryaku-ji Temple grew in power, there was a movement to {{ill|lt=syncretize Shinto and Buddhism|Q2279088|qid=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} with Hiyoshi Shrine, and the deity of Hiyoshi Shrine was {{ill|lt=Tang Dynasty|Q9683|qid=Q9683|1=Tang dynasty}} {{jalink|Tendai sect|Q6 It came to be called {{ill|lt=Sanno Gongen|Q11470054|qid=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}} after Sanno Genjishin, who was enshrined at {{ill|lt=Tendaisan Kokuseiji Temple|Q2509232|qid=Q2509232|1=Guoqing Temple}}, the main temple of 68188}}. Hiyoshi Shrine was established in the 4th year of the {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|qid=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} year ({{ill|lt=880|Q23618|qid=Q23618|1=880}}) as the enshrined deity of Omiya. In 7}}2 ({{ill|lt=1183|Q19723|qid=Q19723|1=1183}}), the deities enshrined in Ninomiya were each given the rank of {{ill|lt=Senior First Rank|Q11123258|qid=Q11123258|1=Senior First Rank}}. In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}", it is ranked as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}.Hiyoshi Shrine gradually became integrated with Enryakuji Temple, and in the middle of the Heian period, {{ill|lt=Jinguji|Q1074971|qid=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}} was built deep inside Mt. Hachioji. Also, along the {{ill|lt=approach|Q667783|qid=Q667783|1=Sandō}} of Hiyoshi Shrine, village temples of Enryakuji Temple began to be lined up. As the Tendai sect spread throughout the country, the {{ill|lt=Sanno faith|Q11470037|qid=Q11470037|1=Sannō Faith}} of Hiyoshi shrines also spread, and Hiyoshi shrines were solicited and built all over the country, and it is said to have had a great influence on the establishment of the modern {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai doctrine|1=Tiantai}}. In {{ill|lt=Tenkei|Q1327340|qid=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}}5 years ({{ill|lt=942 years|Q23740|qid=Q23740|1=942}}), the root {{ill|lt=Tahoto|Q2451601|qid=Q2451601|1=Tahōtō}} is erected. In August of {{ill|lt=Chorei|Q1193519|qid=Q1193519|1=Chōryaku}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1039|Q19341|qid=Q19341|1=1039}}), it was made one of the lower eight shrines of the later {{ill|lt=22 shrines|Q215320|qid=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}}. In the Hiyoshi-sha kanban published in 1081, the theory that Omiya's three-wheeled deity, Tenchicho, was promoted {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}. This is the first reliable historical material for this theory {{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}.{{ill|lt=Kaho|Q1193280|qid=Q1193280|1=Kahō}}2 ({{ill|lt=1095|Q19480|qid=Q19480|1=1095}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=forced appeal|1=UNKNOWN}}, and this incident is said to be an event that marks the beginning of the history of Mikoshi-furi at Enryaku-ji Temple and tells of the greatness of the divine power of Hiyoshi Shrine {{Sfn|Hayakawa et al.|2017|p=225}}. Mino Province Governor {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsuna|Q11564276|qid=Q11564276|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsuna}} has complained to the Imperial Court about the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=evil monk|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q1351234|qid=Q1351234|1=Kokushi (official)}} of the Sanmon Gate of Mt. Hiei, where he committed evil deeds in his country. When Minamoto no Yoshitsuna was thought to have started an outrageous invasion of neighboring duchies), he appealed to the Imperial Court and pursued them based on a proclamation, and some of the evil monks died in the process, and the members unilaterally held that Minamoto no Yoshitsuna was at fault and called for his banishment, carrying a mikoshi (portable shrine) and making a strong case against the capital {{Sfn|Hayakawa et al.|2017|p=224}}. In order to prevent this, the Imperial Court dispatched MINAMOTO no Yoshitsuna and {{ill|lt=MINAMOTO no Yoriharu|Q11564356|qid=Q11564356|1=Minamoto no Yoriharu}} to defend it. At that time, it is said that the mikoshi of the ten Zen masters was shot with arrows, causing multiple casualties. Enryaku-ji Temple and Hiyoshi-sha Shrine ceased their strong appeal and withdrew, but after that Enryaku-ji Temple cursed Fujiwara no Moromichi, the master of Minamoto no Yoshitsuna, and in 3 years ({{ill|lt=Chengde|Q1193901|qid=Q1193901|1=Jōtoku}} ink|1099|Q19496}}) After Moromichi died in June, Enryaku-ji Temple advertised that the mikoshi of Hiyoshi Shrine had divine power, and for the imperial court, the mikoshi of Hiyoshi Shrine became an object of fear. Since then, Enryaku-ji Temple and Hiyoshi-sha Shrine have frequently used this mikoshi to carry out mikoshi movements and make strong appeals, more than 40 times during the 370-odd years from the Heian period to the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|qid=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. In Volume 1 of "{{ill|lt=The Tale of the Heike|Q830740|qid=Q830740|1=The Tale of the Heike}}," {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|qid=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa|1=Emperor Shirakawa}} wrote, (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q27218|Water of the Kamo River}}, {{ill|lt=Sugoroku|Q1472906|qid=Q1472906|lt=Sugaroku Dice|1=Sugoroku}}, and Yamaboshi, there is an anecdote that he lamented, ``I am not worthy of my heart,'' and even the Pope, who had immense power, was unable to control it.Since the beginning of the Shirakawa {{ill|lt=Insei|Q865416|qid=Q865416|1=Cloistered rule}} period, there has been a rapid increase in riots and strong suits at temples and shrines, including the Sanmon gate of Mt. Hiei, and it has been pointed out that the reason for this is likely to be Cloistered Emperor Shirakawa's intervention in human affairs within the temples.{{Sfn|Hayakawa et al.|2017|p=230}} Also, during this period, both manors and koryo tried to maintain and expand their land boundaries by force, and armed conflicts between manors and koryo frequently occurred {{Sfn|Hayakawa et al.|2017|p=230}}. Temples and shrines were the lords of manors, and conflicts between temples and shrines during this period and the national guard of public domain had a background to land disputes over tax collection {{Sfn|Hayakawa et al.|2017|p=230}}. In Hiyoshi Shrine's mikoshi-furi (forced appeal), it was customary for the three mikoshis of the Juzenshi, Hachioji, and guest to move to the Nemoto-chudo Hall, and after a while, the mikoshi of the remaining Kami-shichi shrines moved. It is known that the requests of the people were often fulfilled when there were three lanterns, and mikoshis to Kyoto were sometimes carried out with these three lanterns {{Sfn|Hayakawa et al.|2017|p=217}}.Enryaku-ji Temple began to preach {{ill|lt=Sanno Shinto|Q4407806|qid=Q4407806|1=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} by combining the belief in Sanno Gongen with the teachings of the Tendai sect. The most reliable Sanno Shinto literature is ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotenki|1=UNKNOWN}}'', and it is thought that the original form was established in the first half of the 13th century {{Sfn|Sueki|2003|pp=32-33}}. In "Kotenki," from a doctrinal point of view, ``Sannoji,'' which teaches the theory that Shaka = Omiya's suijaku, is not in the original version and was added later, but it is thought to have been established in the early to mid-13th century {{Sfn|Sueki|2003|pp=32-33}}. The Honji Suijaku theory of "Sannoji" is based on {{ill|lt=Late Law|Q510060|qid=Q510060|1=Mappō}} an evil view of the world, and it is not only that it is the last law, but also that Japan, a small country within a small country, is a place of stupid and rootless people. Since the Buddha never appeared in the world and the Japanese people are not even subject to sermons and indoctrination, it is argued that it is necessary for the Buddha to appear in the form of a god. {{ill|lt=Masamoto (Japan)|Q1194244|qid=Q1194244|lt=Some of the shrine buildings were burnt down in the first year of Shogen|1=Shōgen}} ({{ill|lt=1259|Q5507|qid=Q5507|1=1259}}), but during the Muromachi period, Sanno Shinto became more and more popular, and the shrine flourished, with 108 shrines within the precincts and 108 {{ill|lt=Sessuesha|Q1071160|qid=Q1071160|lt=Setsusha and Suesha|1=Setsumatsusha}} lined up outside the precincts.From the end of {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|qid=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}} to the Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}}, in "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamake Yoshoki|1=UNKNOWN}}," which is thought to have been compiled by Yoshigen, a priest of Eizan from the end of the 13th century to the middle of the 14th century, Ninomiya (Kohiei) is the Tokodateson of the country, and {{ill|lt=Hachioji Gongen|Q1139161 5|qid=Q1139161 5|lt=Hachioji}} is unified and identified with {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Omikami|Q455602|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}'s {{ill|lt=Covenant of Amaterasu and Susano|Q11284880|qid=Q11284880|lt=Eight Princes|1=Five Male and Three Female Deities}}, and the ten Zen masters are united with Qionghuong-son, and the gods of Hiyoshi are {{ill|lt=Heaven's creation. (Japanese mythology)|Q6158445|qid=Q6158445|lt=The myth of the creation of heaven and earth|1=Japanese creation myth}}, and also {{ill|lt=Ise Shinto|Q4205212|qid=Q4205212|1=Ise faith}} {{Sfn|Sueki|2003|p=45}}. In this book, the theory of Honji Suijaku is deepened by relating it to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Three Noble Truths|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Tendai sect, and by integrating and identifying the gods of Hiyoshi with other gods, it deepens the interconnectedness of the gods like a web. However, such work was also carried out in {{ill|lt=Ryobu Shinto|Q4402813|qid=Q4402813|1=Ryobu Shinto}}, and was a characteristic of medieval syncretic Shinto {{Sfn|Sueki|2003|pp=45-46}}. Furthermore, from the end of the Kamakura period to the period of the Nanbokucho period, Japan-centeredness increased in Ryobu Shinto and Ise Shinto, and a similar trend was seen in Sanno Shinto, which was part of the Tendai system. In 1319], based on the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honkaku Thought|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Tendai sect, the anti-Honji Suijaku theory was preached, which held that Japan's gods were the original source and Shakyamuni was the manifestation of God.Since the Heian period, it had flourished along with Enryaku-ji Temple, but at the end of the Middle Ages, the {{ill|lt=Muromachi Shogunate|Q1154406|qid=Q1154406|1=Ashikaga shogunate}} declined and the world was in turmoil, and Hiyoshi Shrine, like other shrines such as Ise Jingu, was in considerable decline {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|p=27}}. In 1493, it was set on fire during the {{ill|lt=Tokusei Uprising|Q11490312|qid=Q11490312|1=tokusei-ikki}} in Shiga, and its scale gradually decreased, with only the rituals centered around Sanno 21 Shrines still being performed {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=27-28}}.{{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|qid=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}} and {{ill|lt=Nagamasa Asai|Q1142446|qid=Q1142446|1=Azai Nagamasa}} and {{ill|lt=Yoshikage Asakura|Q1322768|qid=Q1322768|1=Asakura Yoshikage}} are in conflict. During the middle of the war, Oda Nobunaga told the Hieizan Sanmon to stop aiding Asai and Asakura and that if they refused, he would burn them down, but the Hieizan Sanmon refused {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|p=27}}. {{ill|lt=Motogame|Q1145064|qid=Q1145064|1=Genki (era)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1571|Q6666|qid=Q6666|1=1571}}), Oda Nobunaga's burning of Mt. Hiei (1571)|Q10748887|The burning of Mt. Hiei}} Hiyoshi Shrine was all burnt down to ashes, and medieval Hiyoshi Shrine came to an end {{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=89}}. With the help of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Echizen no kami|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shigetora Nagahara|1=UNKNOWN}}, the four members of the Iwaibe clan of the Hiyoshisha shrine narrowly escaped {{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=89}} and Yukimaru Shogenji ( Yukibe Yukimaru) worked diligently to explain the origin of the seven shrines and the vast number of subsidiary shrines and set shrines, their arrangement, and the deities enshrined, as basic materials for the revival of the seven shrines {{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=148-149}} The aim of reconstruction was centered on Shogenji Gyomaru, but during Oda Nobunaga's lifetime, reconstruction did not materialize, and there was a gap of 10 years until reconstruction began. After Nobunaga's death (1582), Mt. Hiei Sanmon and Hiyoshi Shrine all went straight to reconstruction {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=33-35}}. The buildings that can be seen today are from the {{ill|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|Q319531|qid=Q319531|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} to the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}14 years ({{jalink|1586|Q6 {{Cite news |url=http://hiyoshitaisha.jp/nishihongu/|title=1,350th anniversary of the enshrinement of Nishimoto Shrine|date=|publisher=Hiyoshi Taisha|accessdate=2019-06-11}}. Ceremonies were also arranged by Shogenji Iegyomaru {{Sfn|Miyaie|2007|p=106}}. The Sanno Festival restarted in 1582, 11 years after the burning, but a large sakaki tree was used instead of a mikoshi {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|p=36}}. After Nobunaga's death, {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|qid=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} and {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} were devoted to the Sanno religion, and structures such as the Hiyoshi Mihashi Bridge were built during this period. In this period, the influence of {{ill|lt=Yoshida Shinto|Q1760351|qid=Q1760351|1=Yoshida Shintō}} was already visible, and by the Keicho era, the oratory of the Sanno Festival had been changed to the Yoshida Shinto style {{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=102}}.With the establishment of the Tokugawa shogunate, Enryaku-ji Temple was deprived of the real power of the Tendai sect by Edo under Ieyasu's trusted Tendai priest {{ill|lt=Tenkai|Q1142310|qid=Q1142310|1=Tenkai}}, and the Hiyoshi-sha shrine family was in financial difficulty. It seems that life was not easy for the Kisushita-Sogenji family, who used 11 koku and another 11 koku to cover the expenses for major events, and distributed the remaining 60 koku to 19 priests.{{Sfn|Murayama|1973|pp=498-499}} }}, he hoped that {{ill|lt=Prince Imperial Prince Yoshiko|Q10930627|qid=Q10930627|1=Gyōjo-hosshinnō}}, who was the head of the Tendai daishu, would agree to his request for an increase in the number of shrines and territories, but it was not granted {{Sfn|Murayama|1973|pp=498-499}}. In the latter half of the 17th century, Hiyoshi Shrine was losing not only its ritual rights but also its management rights to Enryaku-ji Temple, and there was also animosity toward {{ill|lt=Sanno Shinto#Sanno Issumi Shinto|Q4407806|qid=Q4407806|lt=Sanno Issumi Shinto|1=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}}, which enshrined Tokugawa Ieyasu as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tosho Daigongen|1=UNKNOWN}}. Hiyoshi Shrine's chiefs tried to counter this ideologically by adopting Yoshida Shinto, which was a syncretism of Shintoism and Buddhism, but was more pure Shinto than Sanno Shinto.In 1684, several priests of Hiyoshi Shrine who were devoted to Yoshida Shinto conspired to create a statue of the deity that was the object of worship at Sanno Seven Shrines (at that time, the object of worship for the shrine's deity was {{jalink |Honji Suijen|Q3080781}} Most of the Buddhist statues {{Sfn|Keishuro|2007|p=56}}) were taken out and burned, according to the Yoshida Shinto religion, which is said to be the "one and only god." Based on this philosophy, he argued that ``Hiyoshi's Shinto is the only Shinto, no monks were allowed into the shrine grounds, and there were no statues of gods from the beginning,'' and he abolished the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism at Hiyoshi Shrine. {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=88-91}}{{Sfn|Murayama|1973|p=499}}{{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=89}}. As a result of the investigation in Edo, it was revealed that there had been a conspiracy by the Shaji and others, and the seven Shaji and others who were involved under the instructions of Rinnoji Monzeki (the head of the Tendai sect in Edo) were exiled or exiled, the cliques were also punished, and the Seigenji family and the Kishita family, which were seven shrine families, were killed. The shrine and family members lost all authority except to serve the festival. Buddha statues and other items have been restored . Ken Sagai believes that Hiyoshi Shrine's fierce {{ill|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|Q702298|qid=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}, which was carried out ahead of the rest of the country during {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, was due to the accumulation of deep-seated dissatisfaction and antipathy of Hiyoshi Shrine against the rule of Enryakuji Temple, and that this incident is the source of this. {{Sfn|Sagai|1992|pp=88-91}}, and Oriental culture researcher {{ill|lt=Alex Carr|Q4717298|qid=Q4717298|1=Alex Kerr (Japanologist)}} states, ``The priests' anger toward Buddhism became a foreshadowing of the Meiji government's suppression of Buddhism ``Haibutsu Kishaku'' that would occur 200 years later.''.Although Yoshida Shinto was superficially wiped out from Hiyoshi Shrine due to the Jokyo Incident, Yoshida Shinto practices were practiced within the shrine family, and it was also transmitted to nearby shrine families {{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=102}}. Additionally, there was an influx of Miwa-ryu Shinto from the {{ill|lt=Ryobe Shinto|Q4402813|qid=Q4402813|1=Ryobu Shinto}} lineage of the {{ill|lt=Shingon sect|Q550182|qid=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} lineage, and Hiyoshi Shrine was not exclusively Sanno Ichimi Shinto {{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=102}}. It is thought that the Hiyoshisha family advocated Miwa-ryu Shinto because of the theory that ``Omiya = Miwa-gami'', but the circumstances are not well understood {{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=102}}. In this way, at Hiyoshi Shrine in early modern times, three Shinto styles, Sanno Kazumi Shinto, Yoshida Shinto, and Ryobu Shinto, Miwa-ryu Shinto, have been passed down, and there are also unique contents that are a mixture of the three styles {{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=102}}. The influence of {{ill|lt=Kokugaku|Q855159|qid=Q855159|1=Kokugaku}} can also be seen, and at the end of the Edo period, Narimasa Sōgenji, Shigenaka Kishita, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shigekuni Kishita|1=UNKNOWN}} were known as Kokugaku {{Sfn|Sato|1990|p=102}}. ===After the Meiji era=== The religious policy of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji government|1=UNKNOWN}} was based on Shigekuni Kishita, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tetsutane Hirata|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Gendo Yano|Q6538145|qid=Q6538145|1=Yano Harumichi}}, {{jalink|Great Powers} He was under the influence of Shintoists of the {{ill|lt=Retro Shinto|Q11489378|qid=Q11489378|1=Fukko Shinto}} lineage, including Takamasa|Q6535446}} and {{ill|lt=Rokuninbu Zehka|Q6537708|qid=Q6537708|1=Yoshika Mutobe}}. However, this Retro Shinto is a Shinto theory that was developed by his disciple {{ill|lt=Atsutane Hirata|Q2572749|qid=Q2572749|1=Hirata Atsutane}} after the death of {{ill|lt=Norinaga Motoori|Q384647|qid=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}}, and which was developed by Confucianism and Buddhism. It was characterized by severe criticism of the ``Shinto'' and denial of syncretic Shinto (the syncretization of Shinto and Buddhism), and sought to return to the Shinto that existed before the introduction of Confucianism and Buddhism.{{Sfn|Kamio|2003|p=4}} These Hirata Shintoists aimed to separate Shinto and Buddhism and make Shinto the state religion through the government's religious policy. {{Sfn|Kamio|2003|p=4}}{{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=157-158}} issued the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu Separation Order|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinbutsu Separation Order|1=UNKNOWN}}, 1868).In response to this, a violent and destructive anti-Buddhist movement arose throughout Japan, but the impetus for the destruction was the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshida Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Kyoto City|Q34600|qid=Q34600|1=Kyoto}}) had a ``Kamui-tai'' consisting of 40 Shinto priests, including {{ill|lt=Priests|Q11589454|qid=Q11589454|1=Shinkan (official)}} (including the Iwaibe clan's Shogenji Kishu, Shogenji Narichika, and Kishita Seigan), and the {{ill|lt=Sakamoto Village] who followed them. (Shiga Prefecture)|Q17228606|qid=Q17228606|lt=Sakamoto Village|1=Sakamoto}} An act of vandalism at Hiyoshi Shrine by dozens of villagers. At that time, the relationship between the priests of Enryaku-ji Temple and the priests of Hiyoshi Shrine was not a good one, and things spiraled out of control as the priests of the shrine took advantage of the Shinto and Buddhism separation ordinance. There is no other example of such violent anti-Buddhism, and Tadashi Nojiri of the Nara National Museum points out that ``a large part depends on the personality of the mastermind (Shigekuni Kishita)'' {{Sfn|Nojiri|2017|p=89}}. }}, they destroyed and burned the treasures displayed at Hiyoshi Shrine, such as Buddhist statues, Buddhist vessels, Buddhist utensils, and sutras, and the number is said to number in the thousands, and all seven shrines of Hiyoshi Shrine, including Juzenshi Shrine, fell victim to their violence.{{Sfn|Takahashi|2019|p= 9}}{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=157}}[https://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/kenseishiryo/kakonotenjishiryo/10721.html [Column] The trajectory of the birth of a "national treasure"] Shiga Prefectural Archives (December 8, 2017) Accessed October 27, 2020 ``On the 28th day of the month, an order prohibiting the mixing of Shinto and Buddhism was issued, and Shigekuni Kishita, the judge of the Hiei Shrine Shrine Shrine, Shigekuni Kinoshita, the chief priest of the same shrine, Genji Kiho, etc., announced that it was necessary to investigate the body of worship in order to separate Shinto and Buddhism, and asked the three executives of the shrine to hand over the key to the shrine. In response, Shigekuni and others led over 40 people including Akashimisaki Shrine in Harima Province, Sanage Shrine in Mikawa Province, Shimosuwa Shrine in Shinano Province, Nakajima Hachimangu Shrine in Nakajima Province, Tenkutsudo Kaisha Shrine in Mimasaka Province, and farmers from Sakamoto Village to Hiyoshi Shrine. They destroyed the shrine buildings, removed the objects of worship, and burned the Buddhist statues, sutra scrolls, Buddhist utensils, etc. at Omiya Shrine, and at Ninomiya, Seimako, Hachioji, Guest, Juzenji, and Sannomiya shrines, they burned the deities, the original Buddha, sutra scrolls, Buddhist utensils, and crocodile mouths, etc., or carried them to the shrine houses, causing a disturbance in the mountains. On the 10th, he issued an edict forbidding violent behavior by priests, and disposing of Buddhist statues and Buddhist utensils in shrines only after requesting permission; and on the 13th, he admonished the monks of Enryaku-ji Temple not to be involved in festivals at Hiyoshi Shrine.'' {{Sfn|Kamio|2003|pp=15-16}}}} Shigekuni Kishita carried out acts of destruction using the Shinto and Buddhism Hanshin Ordinance that he himself had created as a shield, but he went beyond the exclusion of Buddhist-related items from shrines as stated in the edict and went too far, so the Meiji government took over his authority and removed him from the shrine. He was warned not to vent his anger and was temporarily imprisoned by the government.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=157-158}}{{efn|The Meiji government did not weaken the momentum of the Shinbutsu Hanryo. Shigekuni Kishita was also a close friend of {{ill|lt=Tomomi Iwakura|Q428200|qid=Q428200|1=Iwakura Tomomi}}, and part of the Meiji government tacitly approved of this violent anti-Buddhism movement.{{Sfn|Takahashi|2019|p=9}}. }}. This violent incident was the beginning of the spread of {{ill|lt=Haitbutsu-kishaku|Q702298|qid=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} across the country. Regarding this {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shinto-Buddhist separation riot at Hiyoshi Shrine in the first year of the Meiji era|1=UNKNOWN}}, Masato Sato analyzed the trends at Hiyoshi Shrine during {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Keio|1=UNKNOWN}} years (the end of the Edo period). ``The History of the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism at Hiyoshi Shrine - Focusing on the Period up to the 4th Year of Keio'' (1988) has been evaluated as a very important research result {{Sfn|Takechi|2011|p=64}}.Hiyoshi Shrine took the initiative to wipe out its Buddhist influences, became independent from Enryakuji Temple, and changed its name to Hiyoshi Shrine. Due to their vandalism, Hiyoshi Shrine was no longer under the control of Enryaku-ji Temple, and in the first year of the Meiji era (Meiji 1), when the Shinto and Buddhism ban order was issued, the modern Sanno Festival, which excludes Buddhist elements, was held for the first time, but all the priests and lower-ranking priests with the status of priests (shrine priests) who served the seven shrines returned to secular life and participated, and Enryaku-ji monks were not allowed to participate {{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=157}}. Shigekuni Kishita and others went further to exclude Buddhism, and among the seven shrines, they renamed the shrines of {{ill|lt=Ten Zen Masters|Q123524325|qid=Q123524325|1=Jūzenji}}, Seimako, and Hachioji, which they felt were Buddhist {{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=158}}.It is extremely likely that in 1869 (Meiji 2), the year after the Shinbutsu Hanronryo, a person believed to be Shigekuni Kishita forged a historical document called ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hiyoshisha Negiguchi Densho|1=UNKNOWN}}''. This historical document is now considered to be a forgery.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}{{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}. "Hiyoshi Shanegiguchidensho" is said to be a 11th century history written by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gyōgenji Yukimaru|1=UNKNOWN}} in the 16th century {{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}, and Shigekuni Shigeshita based this on {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276 944|qid=Q276 944|lt=Changing the deities enshrined}} except for 944|Oomi Takakami}} (described later) {{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=148-149}}{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=172}}. These enshrined deities were first mentioned in a document called ``Enshrined Deities and Kanjo Nenki etc.'' submitted to Otsu Prefecture in early 1880 by a person believed to be Shigekuni Kishita {{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=159-160}}. In addition, because the name of Oyamakui no Kami is found in the "Kojiki" and the Meiji government placed importance on the tradition that Omiya's solicitation was due to {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|qid=Q449943|1=Saichō}}, Higashi Hongu (Ninomiya) was made the main deity, and the deities enshrined at Nishi Hongu (Omiya) and Higashi Hongu (Ninomiya) were switched {{Sfn|Okada|2003|p=21}}. Along with the complete separation of Hiyoshi Shrine and Enryakuji Temple due to the separation of Shinto and Buddhism, this spurred the revision and abolition of ancient Shinto rituals and rituals {{Sfn|Okada|2003|pp=20-21}}.In {{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}} ({{ill|lt=4th year of Meiji|Q11512280|qid=Q11512280|1=Meiji – year 4}}), Nishimoto Shrine became {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system#government shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. {{ill|lt=1928|Q19715|qid=Q19715|1=1928}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}3), Higashihongu became Kanpei Taisha . In 1942 (after the start of {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|qid=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}), the deities enshrined in the main shrines of the east and west were replaced.{{Sfn|Okada|2003|pp=20-21}}. After {{ill|lt=Pacific War|Q184425|qid=Q184425|1=Pacific War}}, in {{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|qid=Q5165|1=1948}} (Showa 23), it was added to {{ill|lt=Beppon Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. In 1989, research revealed that ``Hiyoshisha Negiguchidensho'' was a forgery.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}{{Sfn|Yoshida|2024|p=190}}.{{ill|lt=2006|Q2021|qid=Q2021|1=2006}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} June 7, 2018), {{jalink|Act on Special Measures Concerning the Preservation of Historic Landscapes in Ancient Capitals|Q1151642|Special Preservation Areas for Historic Landscapes [https://www.mlit.go.jp/crd/park/joho/database/toshiryokuchi/rekishi_fudo/hyou2_shitei.html Designation status of Historic Landscape Conservation Areas and Historic Landscape Special Conservation Districts (as of March 31, 2017)]{{ill|lt=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism|Q1376196|qid=Q1376196|1=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism}}. {{ill|lt=2015|Q2002|qid=Q2002|1=2015}} (2015) {{ill|lt=April 24th|Q2529|qid=Q2529|1=April 24}}, certified as {{ill|lt=Japanese Heritage|Q18382798|qid=Q18382798|1=Japan Heritage}} as a component cultural property of "{{ill|lt=Lake Biwa|Q200239|qid=Q200239|1=Lake Biwa}} and its waterside landscape - water heritage of prayer and living"{{Cite web|Japanese book|url=https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ja/stories/story008/ |title=Lake Biwa and its waterside landscape - water heritage of prayer and life |publisher={{ill|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs|Q1009519|qid=Q1009519|1=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} |accessdate=2020-09-20 }}. Currently, monks from Enryakuji Temple also participate in the annual festival at Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine.On the north side of the entrance to the precincts are {{ill|lt=Former Chikurin-in Garden|Q11511113|qid=Q11511113|1=Former Chikurin-in Garden}}, which was originally Chikurin-in Temple, a village temple on Mt. Hiei, and {{ill|lt=Gupoji|Q60985366|qid=Q60985366|1=Gupoji}}, which is connected to the former three great masters {{ill|lt=Ryogen|Q543930|qid=Q543930|1=Ryōgen}}. === Faith before the Meiji era === {{main|Sanno Faith}} [[File:Top script detail, from- Hie Sanno Miya Mandala (Nara National Museum) (cropped).jpg|thumb|800px|center|Top of Hiyoshi Mountain Royal Palace Mandala on silk (c. 1334-1392). The enshrined deity of Sanno 21 Shrines, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Honji|1=UNKNOWN}} Buddha, {{ill|lt=seed|Q40763|qid=Q40763|1=Seed}}. The top three gods of Sanno, the Sanno Sansho (lower center), the Ten Zen Masters, and the Small Zen Masters are in the form of monks, meaning that these gods are monks, which is quite different from the concept of Shinto gods after {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|qid=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} in the Meiji period.{{Sfn|Kuroda|2003|p=88}} ]] It used to be said that there were 108 shrines within the shrine grounds and 108 shrines outside. The 21 main shrines are collectively called ``Sanno 21 Shrines'' and are also called ``Hiyoshi Okami''.``Deities enshrined (various theories, etc.)'' on the table are various theories of deities enshrined, including the main deities that were enshrined before the ordinance separating Shinto and Buddhism (not all theories are listed) {{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|pp=16-17}}. In the past, {{ill|lt=Sanno Faith|Q11470037|qid=Q11470037|1=Sannō Faith}} and {{ill|lt=Sanno Shinto|Q4407806|qid=Q4407806|1=Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} were popular. Sanno Shinto is based on the doctrines of the Tendai sect, and the doctrines of Sanno Shinto can be said to be the same as those of the Tendai sect, centered on the faith in ``{{ill|lt=Lotus Sutra|Q861212|qid=Q861212|1=Lotus Sutra}}'' and {{ill|lt=Shakyamuni|Q9441|qid=Q9441|1=The Buddha}}{{Sfn|Sugawara|2001|pp=192-193}}. Similar to the doctrine of the Tendai sect, {{ill|lt=Shaka Nyorai|Q11644537|qid=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}} (Ohiei Myojin) is in the middle, and on either side are {{ill|lt=Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773|qid=Q854773|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} (Ninomiya) and {{ill|lt=Amitabha Tathagata|Q236242|qid=Q236242|1=Amitābha}} (Seishin). These three deities are the principal deities of the West Pagoda, East Pagoda, and Yokogawa of Mt. Hiei, and are a combination that plays an important role in the Tendai sect. In the Sanno faith and Tendai sect, the three deities Ohiei (Omiya, presently Nishihongu), Kohiei (Ninomiya, presently Higashihongu), and Seimako (now Usamiya) are known as the Sanno Sanho and are revered as the gods representing Mt. Hiyoshi. |url=https://artscape.jp/artword/5555/|access-date=2025-04-16|author=|website=Shinjoji|publisher={{ill|lt=Dainippon Printing|Q1157511|qid=Q1157511|1=Dai Nippon Printing}}}}. Seimako was the worshiper of {{ill|lt=Usa Jingu|Q715632|qid=Q715632|lt=Usa Hachiman|1=Usa Jingū}}, and is also said to be the child of Omiya and Ninomiya {{Sfn|Miyaie|2007|pp=105-106}}. Originally, Sanno Sansho was considered to be the three-wheeled deity of .There is also a belief that {{ill|lt=Sanno Gongen|Q11470054|qid=Q11470054|1=Sannō Gongen}} and {{ill|lt=Jūzenshi|Q123524325|qid=Q123524325|1=Jūzenji}} Gongen (kami in the form of a monk or child) are valued; The first person that {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|qid=Q449943|1=Saichō}} encountered when he entered Mt. Hiei was Reido (an incarnation of the Ten Zen Master Gongen{{efn|In the Tendai sect, the term ``one child, two Sannos'' was widely used in the late Kamakura period, and 1414 In the ``Gonjin Sho'', which has a colophon from 2011, the ``Ichigo'' in ``Ichigo Nisanno'' are said to be the ten Zen masters {{Sfn|Koyama|2007|p=25}}{{Sfn|Koyama|2007|p=42}} }}), and the next one to appear was the great one. The theory that ``One Child, Two Sannos'' was spread that he was a kajin (an incarnation of Sanno Gongen) and had a very important meaning for the Tendai sect. {{ill|lt=Chigo|Q30940647|qid=Q30940647|1=Chigo}}, which is based on the sacred view of As it was said, "Chigo) Nite Goza (Owashimasu)" (Shokoku Mikitsu Sei Monogatari, 1387), the Tendai sect's secret rite "Chigo (Empowerment |Q75799}}") Chigo who received Chigo Kanjo (Chigo Kanjo) is said to be the embodiment of the Ten Zen Master Gongen, the same body as {{ill|lt=Kannon Bodhisattva|Q193849|qid=Q193849|1=Avalokiteśvara}}, and is considered to be the sacred representative of Mt. Hiei. {{Sfn|Koyama|2007|p=26}}{{Sfn|Hiramatsu|2007|pp=110-111}}{{Sfn|Matsuoka|1991|p=129}}. As the worship of Chigo increased, the phrase ``One child, two Sanno'' changed to an expression that placed Chigo higher than Sanno Gongen, and in the doctrine of Sanno Shinto, the ten Zen masters were placed in the highest position. {{Sfn|Matsuoka|1991|p=129}}{{Sfn|Porath|2022}}{{efn|The idea that Chigo is the incarnation of the Ten Zen Master Gongen dates back to the mid-Heian period It was established under the influence of the {{ill|lt=Honji Suijaku|Q3080781|qid=Q3080781|1=Honji suijaku}} ideology that spread in the {jalink|Kannon Bodhisattva|Q193849}} It was converted into a human being, as it was said to be a sutra.{{Sfn|Hiramatsu|2007|pp=110-111}}. In the Tendai sect, it was believed that Chigo, who became a sacred being through Kokanjo (Chigo Kanjo), served as the sexual partner of the monk, and through sexual love gave the monk salvation. }}.The guest shrine is said to have been built by Hiyoshi Shrine's Miyako (lower shaman of Hiyoshi Shrine), who had been visiting Mt. Hakusan for many years. Hachioji and Sannomiya are said to have appeared at the Golden Rock on the summit of Mt. Hachioji (Mt. Kohiei), and there are shrines on either side of the Golden Rock, so they can be said to be twin shrines. {{ill|lt=Sanno Festival (Otsu City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Sanno Festival}}'s ``Miare Shinto ritual'' represents the birth of the male and female deities of Hachioji and Sannomiya, and this Mikogami was enshrined at ``Owome'' (currently Ubuya Shrine), but Tendai {{ill|lt=Shugendo|Q1483875 Due to the relationship between|qid=Q1483875 Due to the relationship between|lt=Shugen}} and {{ill|lt=Kumano|Q6443450|qid=Q6443450|1=Kumano region}}, the suijaku theory that Kumano {{ill|lt=Wakaichi Oji|Q11616186|qid=Q11616186|1=Nyakuichi Ōji}} (Kumano Mysterious Douji) was quite strong {{Sfn|Suzuki|2008|p=85}}. ;Western Sanno (current Nishihongu lineage) {| class="wikitable" ! Miwa-kami ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miwa-Myojin|1=UNKNOWN}} of {{ill|lt=Miwa-san|Q860627|qid=Q860627|1=Mount Miwa}} in Yamato, where the dynasty of Emperor Sujin existed) {{efn| In "Hiyoshi Shane Iguchidensho", Oki Takajin is said to be "Omi Takakami at Hiei Yamaguchi Nichidaihieigu Shrine in Miwaza, Yamato Province, on the third day of the third month of Boshin in the 7th year of Emperor Tenchi's imperial edict, and the 8th grandchild Ushimaro of Kamogashima" ||{{ill|lt=Amida Nyorai|Q236242|qid=Q236242|1=Amitābha}}{{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}} |- |Guest {{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}} (Maroud)||Guest Daimyojin.
