Ninomiya Gongen. {{ill|lt=Oyamakui no Kami|Q10935105|qid=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamasue no Omushikami|1=UNKNOWN}} of "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}"). {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunitototachijin|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}}{{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=148-149}}||{{ill|lt=Yakushi Tathagata|Q854773|qid=Q854773|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}{{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}} |- |Hachioji{{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}} (Yaouji)||{{ill|lt=Hachioji Gongen|Q11391615|qid=Q11391615|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}. {{ill|lt=Kunisa Tsuchiko|Q5966692|qid=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} and eight other princes {{Sfn|Miyaie|2007|p=109}}. {{ill|lt=Amaterasu and Susanoo's Covenant|Q11284880|qid=Q11284880|lt=Amaterasu Omikami's five sons and three goddesses (Hachioji)|1=Five Male and Three Female Deities}}. Amaterasu Omikami{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kikkon|1=UNKNOWN}}||{{ill|lt=Senju Kannon|Q10904910|qid=Q10904910|1=Thousand-armed Kannon}}{{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}} |-|Jūzenji||{{ill|lt=Ten Zenji|Q123524325|qid=Q123524325|1=Jūzenji}}Gongen. {{ill|lt=Reigning God|Q521834|qid=Q521834|lt=Arajingami|1=Arahitogami}}{{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}} (A priest with spiritual powers who was able to communicate with Sannogami became Arajingami after his death.{{Sfn|Nanami|1984|p=103}}) {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Qiong Qiongzhen|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|lt=Amagiya no Mikoto|Q2564508|qid=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyayakami|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}||{{ill|lt=Jizo Bodhisattva|Q604687|qid=Q604687|1=Kṣitigarbha}}, {{ill|lt=Miroku Bodhisattva|Q193461|qid=Q193461|1=Maitreya}}, etc.{{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}} |- |Sannomiya{{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}} (Miguu)||{{ill|lt=Tenninhomison|UNKNOWN|lt=Masayago Katsushoku Nitenninhomison}}{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=149}}. The third daughter of Amaterasu Omikami {{Sfn|Miyaie|2007|p=117}}. {{ill|lt=Shirahige Shrine|Q705303|qid=Q705303|lt=Shirahige Myojin|1=Shirahige Shrine}} is recommended {{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}}||{{ill|lt=Fugen Bosatsu|Q868306|qid=Q868306|1=Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)}}, {{ill|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|Q239847|qid=Q239847|1=Vairocana}} etc. |} === About the monkeys in Hiyoshi === In "Yantianki", it is said that the Yellow Emperor's minister {{ill|lt=蒼頡|Q1033199|qid=Q1033199|1=Cangjie}} invented the character "God", and "God" is written as "Shen Erjisu", and this "Mon" is the monkey of Hiyoshi Shrine, and in connection with the Chinese theory of "Five Elements Great Values", it is said that "Shen = It is said that the meaning of ``Monkey'' is embodied in the behavior of the monkey, which has ``ten, earth and two'' and ``jizai na''. This "monkey = god" is the "shin = god" of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=five organs|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=five gods|1=UNKNOWN}} theory (five organs: liver, heart, spleen, lungs, kidney; five gods: soul, god, will, spirit, spirit) of the "Five Elements Great Values". According to the "God" theory, it was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=analogy|1=UNKNOWN}} and syncretized with the king of the gods, the king of the gods, and the monkey (monkey) was considered to be the form of the response to the king of the mountain (Shakyamuni), and the monkey was regarded as the god itself and was worshiped as a "divine body" {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|pp=286-288}}. Sōyō was Shakyamuni Buddha's previous life, and Sōyō is said to have predicted that Shakyamuni Buddha would appear as the mountain king in Hiyoshi, Japan in the future, and used the shape of a monkey to indicate good and bad fortune.{{Sfn|Omori|2019|p=53}} When the Buddha's Hiyoshi sutra was realized, a herd of monkeys gathered on the banks of Hiyoshi Shrine, and {{ill|lt=Yakushi Tathagata|Q854773|qid=Q854773|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} also sutraced at Ninomiya, but it is said that he was a monkey in his previous life {{Sfn|Omori|2019|p=53}}.One of the legends and teachings of Hiyoshi's monkeys is the ``Hiraiho Engi'' in ``Keiran Shuyoshu'', which states that ``The corner of Tenjiku {{jalink|Mt. It is said that the horns of the mountain cloaks were blown away and became Japan's Mt. Hiei, but all these flying peaks were carried by white monkeys on their backs.'' This is the Sanno theory from the Three Kingdoms.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=287}} In this story, the monkey of the mountain king originally served as the guardian of Mt. Reiwashi and was the messenger of the mountain king, and was consecrated as the ``white monkey.''{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=287}}. This is called the ``Tehiro Monkey,'' and it is said that in his death poem, the Tendai monk Shunpan of the Kamakura period swore that after his death he would become a ``Tehiro Monkey'' and preach for the monkey at the shrine of the ten Zen masters (``Keiran Shuyoshu'') {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=287}}. Here, ``Teshiro no Monkey'' is associated with the Ten Zen Masters, and similar examples can be found in other texts as well.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=287}}.{{ill|lt=Chigomonogatari|Q11596296|qid=Q11596296|1=Chigo Monogatari}} (Children's Tale) by Sanno Tokoro, {{ill|lt=Sekkyobushi|Q11631575|qid=Q11631575|1=sekkyōbushi}} ``{{ill|lt=Aigo Waka|Q109602098|qid=Q109602098|1=Aigo Waka}}'' is about a man who takes a dip in Kirifuri Falls on Mt. Hiei after a difficult journey. The story is about a dead boy, Aigo Waka, who after his death is {{ill|lt=reincarnated|Q128593|qid=Q128593|1=Reincarnation}} and enshrined at the Sanno Gongen, but the monkey that he loved is called the ``hand-white monkey'' and disappears in grief over Aigo Waka's death.{{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=288}}. The monkey who adores his master is modeled after a monkey who is an emissary of the mountain king {{Sfn|Yamamoto|1991|p=288}}. Akira Amino states that the great event of Chuushichisha Shrine was once the local {{ill|lt=landlord deity|Q4160872|qid=Q4160872|1=Landlord deity}}, a god syncretized with the monkeys of Hiyoshi, and that due to its deep ties with monkeys, it was as important as Kamishichisha {{Sfn|Amino|2004|pp=196-197}}. The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=big event|1=UNKNOWN}} of Chuushichisha has a deep relationship with monkeys, and from the ``Hiyoshi Sannogon Genji Shinki'' and Chester Peaty's book ``Junirui Uta Gosaki'' (before the first half of the 15th century), there is a deep relationship with monkeys. We can see that the attendants were monkeys, that monkeys and major events were equated, and that major events were recognized by the appearance of monkeys wearing robes and crowns.{{Sfn|Amino|2004|pp=196-197}} Akira Amino argues that the great event is a divine messenger of Sanno, but it is a god syncretized with the monkey of Hiyoshi, and is not confined to the category of a divine messenger.{{Sfn|Amino|2004|pp=196-197}}.It is well known that the divine messenger of Hiyoshi shrine is a monkey, but {{Sfn|Amino|2004|pp=196-197}}, {{ill|lt=Shigeyoshi Omori|Q11436428|qid=Q11436428|1=Shigenori Omori}} {{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga-taisha}}, fox and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari|1=UNKNOWN}}, crow and {{ill|lt=Kumano Sanzan|Q10403700|qid=Q10403700|lt=Kumano|1=Kumano Three Shrines}}, monkey and Hiyoshi This kind of correspondence is famous, but the fact that such a specific animal had a role as a specific {{ill|lt=misaki|Q7607372|qid=Q7607372|lt=messenger of the gods|1=Misaki}} was in the Kamakura period's ``{{jal ink|The Tale of the Heike|Q830740}}' and '{{ill|lt=Genpei Rise and Susuki|Q3020148|qid=Q3020148|1=Genpei Jōsuiki}}' and later {{Sfn|Omori|2019|p=53}}. In "Gonjin-tsho", {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko|1=UNKNOWN}} is compared to the Great Event Gongen, and is said to be the god who protects the Ten Zen Master Gongen (who was equated with Qiong-Kingson) and guides the {{ill|lt=Descent of the Tensons|Q1084733|qid=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}} {{Sfn|Hashimoto|2020|pp=55-56}}. In {{ill|lt=Kose Hoan|Q6561776|qid=Q6561776|1=Oze Hoan}}'s ``{{ill|lt=Taikoki|Q1083343|qid=Q1083343|1=Taikōki}}'', it is said that Toyotomi Hideyoshi was born on January 1st of the year of the Monkey, so his childhood name was ``Hiyoshimaru'' because he received blessings from Hiyoshi Shrine, but this is thought to be a fiction.{{Cit ation|Japanese book|last=Watanabe|first=Seisuke|author-link=Watanabe Seisuke|year=1919|title=Toyotaiko and his family|publisher=Japan Science Promotion Association|page=35-36|url={{NDLDC|953289/51}}}}. === After Meiji === ===Replacement of enshrined deities and new deity system=== {| class="wikitable" ! colspan="2"|Early modern!!colspan="2"|Today |- ! says that the deity enshrined at Ninomiya is Oyamakui's Wakon, and the enshrined deity at Ushionomiya is Oyamakui's Aratama, and Yukimaru Shogenji's theory that ``Kohieizan Daimyojin'' was enshrined in the shadow of Mt. Hachioji even before Ōki no Kami was enshrined at Mt. Even before the deity of Mt. Miwa was enshrined, it was worshiped in the shadow of Mt. Hachioji, and this Kohiei Daimyojin is considered to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kunijotateson|1=UNKNOWN}}, the first deity after the creation of heaven and earth, and the name of Oyamakui is not seen {{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=148-149}}. }} In addition, it is thought to be influenced by Norinaga Motoori's {{ill|lt=Aratama/Watama|Q495568|qid=Q495568|1=Mitama}} theory and the claim that Oyamakui and Tamayorihime are a married couple.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=159-160}}. Norinaga Motoori does not mention the mutual relationship between the four shrines of Hachioji, Sannomiya, Ninomiya, and Juzenshi at all {{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=159-160}}. Shigekuni Kishita also wrote ``{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}'' and {{ill|lt=Norinaga Motoori|Q384647|qid=Q384647|1=Motoori Norinaga}}'s ``{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Kojiki|qid=Kojiki|ltq=Q813031}}''. Oyamakui was enshrined as the deity based on the ``Kiden|Q11410835}}'', but Tagoko Hime-jin, the deity enshrined at {{ill|lt=Munakata Shrine|Q11451876|qid=Q11451876|1=Munakata Shrines}}, {{j alink|Hakusan Hime Shrine|Q704702|Hakusan Hime Shrine}} is the enshrined deity, Hakusan Hime ({{ill|lt=Kikuri Himegami|Q11619891|qid=Q11619891|lt=Kikuri Himegami|1=Kikurihime}}) Neither appear in "Kojiki" or "Kojikiden" {{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=160}}{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=172}}. The shrines in the current Higashihongu shrine grounds are described as ``Oyamakui no Kami's gods that guide the family and life.'' John Breen sees the interrelationship of the four shrines of Hachioji, Sannomiya, Ninomiya, and Juzenji as a product of Shigekuni's imagination, saying, ``In any case, it is these gods (invented by Shigekuni) that are currently being worshiped at Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine. And it can be said with certainty that these gods were ``created'' with the Meiji Restoration.''{{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=159-160}}Boleyn states that although ``Enshrined Deities and Kanjo Nenki'' appear to be fabrications by Shigekuni Kishita, they can also be understood as the result of his search for the true identity of Hiyoshi Shrine in order to return it to its original appearance and its original enshrined deities.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=159-160}} Boleyn also speculates that Shigekuni Kishita may have thought that the shrine was a suitable shrine for Tamayorihime, who had picked up an arrow in the Kamogawa River, since there was a spiritual spring right below the shrine.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|pp=159-160}}. Boleyn considers that the basis for Shigekuni Kishita's claims other than Kui Oyama's appearance in Kojiki is weak or nonexistent {{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}. It was believed that this was based on a historical document called ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hiyoshi Shane Iguchi Densho|1=UNKNOWN}}'' which was written in the 16th century by Yukimaru Ikube, who wrote about the history of the 11th century, but some people, such as {{ill|lt=Yasun Fukui|Q11591380|qid=Q11591380|1=Kōjun Fukui}}, said that it did not seem to be a historical document from that time. It is known that this is a fake book created by a person believed to be Shigekuni Kishita at the end of the Edo period and during the Meiji Restoration.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}{{Sfn|Yoshida |2024|p=190}}{{Efn|The second part (of Masato Sato's ``Research on Hiyoshi Shrine and Sanno Shinto'') examines the establishment of the Sanno Shinto canon and its forgeries. The first chapter is a major accomplishment in questioning the ``Hiyoshisha Negiguchi Densho'', which is considered to be the oldest book on the theory of the Hiyoshi shrine enshrined deity, based on an examination of various books, and denying the authenticity of this book by providing evidence that it was written between the end of the early modern period and the beginning of the Meiji period. ” (Chief reviewer: Waseda University Faculty of Letters, Arts and Sciences, Professor {{ill|lt=Yoshun Okubo|Q11432057|qid=Q11432057|1=Ryōshun Ōkubo}}, Reviewer: Waseda University Faculty of Letters, Arts and Sciences, Professor {{ill|lt=Kenjiro Tsuchida|Q11423791|qid=Q11423791|1=Kenjirō Tsuchida}}, Kokugakuin University Professor Emeritus/Visiting Professor {{ill|lt=Shoji Okada|Q20039179|qid=Q20039179|1=Okada Chuangji}}）{{Cite web |title=Abstract of Doctoral (Literature) Dissertation Review Report “Research on Hiyoshi Shrine and Sanno Shinto”|author Masato Sato=|url=http://hdl.handle.net/2065/00074133|website=Waseda University Repository |publisher=Waseda University |date= |access-date=2025-05-17 }}}}. All descriptions of the gods that Shigekuni Shigekuni enshrined, including Oyamakui, are based on the ``Hiyoshisha Negiguchi Densho,'' and Boleyn commented, ``This forgery called the ``Hiyoshisha Negikuchi Densho'' must be seen as a great success.'' {{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=172}}, Prior to Masato Sato's research, ``Hiyoshisha Negiguchi Densho'' was generally believed to be from before the medieval period, and since the Meiji period, it has been used as the basis for claiming that the deity enshrined at Hiyoshi Shrine is Oyamakui and his wife Tamayorihime.{{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}. The modern Sanno Festival, which is completely different from the Edo period, is based on the ``Hiyoshishanegiguchidensho'' {{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=161}}. The modern Sanno Festival since the Meiji period has become an event in which the couple deities Oyamakui and Tamayorihime interact to give birth to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamobetsu Raijin|1=UNKNOWN}} {{Sfn|Breen|2009|p=145}}. === Current enshrined deity === It consists of two main shrines and the following five sub-shas, which are called Hiyoshi Seven Shrines and Sanno Seven Shrines. The seven shrines are currently divided into the Nishi Hongu system and the Higashi Hongu system {{Sfn|Fukudome|2013|p=16}}. ; Main shrine * Nishimoto Shrine: '''{{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Omi Takajin|1=Ōkuninushi}}''' (same as {{ill|lt=Ōmonushi|Q10936701|qid=Q10936701|1=Ōmononushi}}) * Higashihongu: '''{{ill|lt=Oyama Kuijin|Q10935105|qid=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}''' ;Gosetsusha * Ushio Shrine: Oyamakuijin Aratama - Oyamakuijin {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aratama|1=UNKNOWN}}. Chūai}}||Usamiya precincts |} === Precincts === {{Attribute source|date=August 2025|section=1}} * Nishihongu Main Hall ({{ill|lt=National Treasure|Q1139795|qid=Q1139795|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}) - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|lt=Ōki no Kami|1=Ōkuninushi}}. Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}14 ({{ill|lt=1586|Q6739|qid=Q6739|1=1586}}). Renovated in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}2 ({{ill|lt=1597|Q6790|qid=Q6790|1=1597}}). Kamishichisha (Sanno Shichisha). {{ill|lt=Buddhist services|Q45265996|qid=Q45265996|1=Buddhist rites}} were once held in the lower hall. The {{ill|lt=cypress bark roofing|Q90373477|qid=Q90373477|lt=cypress bark roofing|1=cypress bark roof}}, and the {{ill|lt=roof|Q83180|qid=Q83180|1=Roof}} is {{ill|lt=Hiyoshi-zukuri|Q3138720|qid=Q3138720|1=Hiyoshi-zukuri}}, a style unique to Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine. When viewed from the front, it looks like a {{ill|lt=gambling|Q855025|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}, but its distinctive feature is that the {{ill|lt=eaves|Q163972|qid=Q163972|1=Eaves}} (eaves) at the center of the back are cut up, and this part is vertically cut off. ** Nishihongu Worship Hall ({{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|qid=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) - Rebuilt in 1586. * Nishihongu Tower Gate (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1586. Sacred monkeys are carved in the four corners under the eaves of the tower gate. * Omiya Kamado Shrine - Enshrined deities: Okutsuhiko-kami, Okutsu-hime-kami. Shimoshichisha. * Kochizuka* {{ill|lt=Usa Shrine|Q715632|qid=Q715632|lt=Usa Shrine|1=Usa Jingū}} Main Shrine (Important Cultural Property) - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Takiribime|Q10932870|qid=Q10932870|lt=Tagoko Himejin|1=Tagorihime}}. Founded in {{ill|lt=Ninwa|Q1346317|qid=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} ({{ill|lt=885|Q24894|qid=Q24894|1=885}} - {{ill|lt=889|Q23833|qid=Q23833|1=889}}). Rebuilt in Keicho 3 ({{ill|lt=1598|Q6800|qid=Q6800|1=1598}}). Kamishichisha (Sanno Shichisha). Like the main halls of the Nishi Hongu Shrine and the Higashi Hongu Main Hall, the roof is Hiyoshi-zukuri. ** Usamiya Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1598. * Usawakamiya - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Shitateruhime|Q11307233|qid=Q11307233|lt=Shimoteruhime|1=Shitateruhime}}. Chuushichisha. * Usagamado Shrine - Deities enshrined: Okutsuhikokami, Okutsuhimekami * {{ill|lt=Kehi Shrine|Q11129346|qid=Q11129346|lt=Kehisha|1=Kehi Shrine}} - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|qid=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}. Shimoshichisha. * Treasure store - Tokurazo. * {{ill|lt=Hakusan Shrine|Q11579587|qid=Q11579587|lt=Hakusan Hime Shrine|1=Hakusan Shrine}} Main shrine (Important Cultural Property) - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Kikurihime God|Q11619891|qid=Q11619891|lt=Hakusan Hime God|1=Kikurihime}}. Rebuilt in 1598. Shimoshichisha. * Kōhakusansha Shrine - Enshrined deity: Taiki Takamiko * {{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q692714|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}} - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanokami|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}* {{ill|lt=Kitano Tenmangu Shrine|Q662176|qid=Q662176|lt=Kitano Shrine|1=Kitano Tenmangū}} - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|qid=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * Patience {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Jizo|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Kotodaishu|Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} God * Relief Jizo * Mikoshi storage - Seven mikoshi from each of the Kamishichi shrines (all important cultural properties) are on display. These are not the portable shrines used in the current Sanno Festival. * Sannomiya Yohaisho - Located at the foot of Mt. Hachioji. * Ushio Shrine Yohaisho - Located at the foot of Mt. Hachioji. * Sannomiya Shrine Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) - Enshrined Deity: Kamo Tamayorihime God {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Aratama|1=UNKNOWN}}. Rebuilt in Keicho 4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|qid=Q6803|1=1599}}). Kamishichisha (Sanno Shichisha). It is located near the summit of Mt. Hachioji. ** Sannomiya Shrine Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1599. Connected to the main shrine. {{ill|lt=suspension|Q94062224|qid=Q94062224|1=kakezukuri}}. * Ushio Shrine Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) - Enshrined Deity: Oyama Kuijin Aratama. Founded in the first year of {{ill|lt=Tenki|Q1193591|qid=Q1193591|1=Tengi}} ({{ill|lt=1053|Q19368|qid=Q19368|1=1053}}). Rebuilt in 1595. Kamishichisha (Sanno Shichisha). It is located near the summit of Mt. Hachioji. ** Ushio Shrine Hall of Worship (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1595. Connected to the main shrine. Hanging structure. * Ushimikosha Shrine - Enshrined deity: Yamasue no Omushikami Aratama. Chuushichisha. It is enshrined within the worship hall of Ushio Shrine.* Golden Rock (Kogane no Oiwa) - Also known as Golden Rock. It is said that the god Oyamakui descended there {{ill|lt=Iwaza|Q11587880|qid=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}}. This is the origin of Hiyoshi Taisha. * Okusoja - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Amatsukami/Kunitsukami|Q11442793|qid=Q11442793|lt=Tenjinjizi|1=Amatsukami and Kunitsukami}}. The statue of Dengyo Daishi {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|qid=Q449943|1=Saichō}} is enshrined here. It is the site of the former {{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple|Q1074971|qid=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. It is also the place where Saicho's father, Momoe Mitsukubi, prayed for the birth of his child. It is located further into Mt. Hachioji. * Higashi Hongu Main Hall (National Treasure) - Enshrined deity: Oyamakui. Founded in {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenji|Q314802|qid=Q314802|1=Emperor Tenji}}7 ({{ill|lt=668|Q30116|qid=Q30116|1=668}}). Rebuilt in 1595. Kamishichisha (Sanno Shichisha). Hiyoshizo. It is also the village shrine of Ushio Shrine. {{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|qid=Q7717|1=1868}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} first year) to {{jalink|1928|Q19715} } (Showa 3), the enshrined deity was Oomiwa Shrine ({{ill|lt=Omononushi|Q10936701|qid=Q10936701|1=Ōmononushi}} God), so it was called ``Omiwa Shrine Main Hall''. It used to be located northwest of the current location. The architectural style is similar to the main shrine of Nishimoto Shrine. ** Higashihongu Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in Bunroku 5 ({{ill|lt=1596|Q6785|qid=Q6785|1=1596}}). * Omonoki Shrine (Tangible Cultural Property designated by Otsu City) - Deity enshrined: Dai{{ill|lt=Toshigami|Q3028663|qid=Q3028663|1=Toshigami}}. Chuushichisha. It is located behind the main hall of the Higashi Hongu Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} - Enshrined deity: {{ill|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|lt=Uga no Mitamajin|1=Ukanomitama}}* Ninomiya Kamadono Shrine - Enshrined deities: Okutsuhikokami, Okutsuhimekami. Shimoshichisha. * Divine food place * Shinmoni Shrine - Enshrined deity: Amachikaryusuihime no kami. Chuushichisha. * Kamei Reisui - Former Yugai. * Main shrine of Tree Shrine (Important Cultural Property) - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime|Q56344982|qid=Q56344982|lt=Kamotamayorihime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime}}. Founded in {{ill|lt=Tennin|Q1193599|qid=Q1193599|1=Tennin (era)}}2 ({{ill|lt=1109|Q19515|qid=Q19515|1=1109}}). Rebuilt in 1595. Kamishichisha (Sanno Shichisha). It is also the village shrine of Sannomiya Shrine. There is a spiritual spring {{ill|lt=well|Q43483|qid=Q43483|1=Well}} right below the main shrine. The line of the main hall and hall of worship at Kishita Shrine is perpendicular to the line of the main hall of the Higashihongu shrine, hall of worship, and tower gate. The original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Jūzenji|Q123524325|qid=Q123524325|1=Jūzenji}}, and Kishita Shrine was once called Jūzenshi-sha. ** Kishita Shrine Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1595. * Uchinomiko Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Sartahiko|Q3090037|qid=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahikojin|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}} *Kishita Wakamiya - Enshrined deity: Tamayorihiko-kami. Shimoshichisha. * Mikoshi warehouse * Higashihongu Tower Gate (Important Cultural Property) - Rebuilt during the Tensho and Bunroku periods ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|qid=Q6678|1=1573}} - 1596). * Suga Shrine - Enshrined Deity: Sujinjin{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kikkon|1=UNKNOWN}} * Iwataki Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ichikishima Hime|Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishima Hime no Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}}, {{ill|lt=Tagitsuhime|Q10932592|qid=Q10932592|lt=Tanatsushima Hime no Mikoto|1=Tagitsuhime}}. Shimoshichisha. * Saruiwa - Also called the monkey's spirit stone. It is a rock shaped like a monkey.* Ushigami Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Kamo Kenkaku Mimikoto|Q11634939|qid=Q11634939|lt=Kamo Kenkaku Mimichi|1=Kamotaketsunumi no Mikoto}}, Kotomitate Ushimaro (the founder of the Hiyoshi Shrine family). Shimoshichisha. * Ujinaga Shrine - Enshrined deity: Kien Iwaibe (ancestress of the Shogenji family of Hiyoshi Shrine family) * Yabashira Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Covenant of Amaterasu and Susanoo|Q11284880|qid=Q11284880|lt=Five Sons and Three Goddesses|1=Five Male and Three Female Deities}}. Chuushichisha. * Shogaku-in Temple {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Inari Daimyojin|1=UNKNOWN}} - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Daikuniten|Q1127224|qid=Q1127224|1=Dakini}} * Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine Precincts Kofun Group - There are about 70 {{ill|lt=circular tombs|Q11394747|qid=Q11394747|1=Round Kofun}} from the late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}}. * Stone pagoda - Located to the west of the West Main Shrine tower gate. * Hashidonobashi Bridge - The bridge is cut off in the middle. * {{ill|lt=Gion|Q926312|qid=Q926312|1=Gion}} Stone - Iwaza where {{ill|lt=Gozu Tenno|Q11570247|qid=Q11570247|1=Gozu Tennō}} dwells. * Daiitoku Stone - A spiritual stone in which {{ill|lt=Daitoku Myoo|Q1348317|qid=Q1348317|1=Yamantaka}} resides. * Sanshuden * Shrine office * Gyojasha (Gyojado) * God Monkey House * Shinbasha * Sosha - Enshrines the 11 shrines of Sanno. * Goshinsha - Koyasusha, Kotatsusha * Hiyoshi Kaikan * Saruzuka - One of the group of tombs in the precincts of Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine.* Hiyoshi Mitsuhashi - It is said that {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|qid=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} donated it during the Tensho era ({{ill|lt=1573|Q6678|qid=Q6678|1=1573}} - {{ill|lt=1593|Q6772|qid=Q6772|1=1593}}). The wooden bridge was replaced with the current stone bridge in Kanbun 9 ({{ill|lt=1669|Q7602|qid=Q7602|1=1669}}). In August 1917 ({{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|qid=Q2092|1=1917}}), it became a designated cultural property as Hiyoshi Mitsuhashi. ** Omiya Bridge (Important Cultural Property) - A granite stone bridge that spans the approach to Nishimoto Shrine (Omiya). ** Hasiri Bridge (Important Cultural Property) - A stone bridge used for purification just downstream of Omiya Bridge. ** Ninomiya Bridge (Important Cultural Property) - A granite stone bridge that spans the approach to Higashihongu (Ninomiya). * Hashirii Haraden Shrine - Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|qid=Q13579836|lt=Seoritsuhime|1=Seoritsu-hime}}, {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko Hayaakitsuhime|Q11326733|qid=Q11326733|lt=Soku Kaito Hime|1=Hayakitsuhiko and Hayakitsuhime}}, {{ill|lt=Kebukito no Nushikami|Q86734190|qid=Q86734190|lt=Kebukito no Hime|1=Ibukidonushi no Kami}}, Hayasa Surahime * Hasiriisugi * Hayao Shrine - Enshrined deity: Suzujin. Chuushichisha. It is enshrined on the south side of the entrance to the temple grounds. Across the approach to the shrine is {{ill|lt=Gupoji|Q60985366|qid=Q60985366|1=Gupoji}}, and in front of the front stairs of our entrance is Rokkaku Jizo-do Hall. * Outside the precincts ** Omandokoro (Yoi Miyaba) - Otabisho. It is located to the east of Hiyoshi Taisha. This is the stage for the Yoimiya Otoshi event of the Sanno Festival. ** Ubuya Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Raijin|Q11634943|qid=Q11634943|lt=Kamobetsu Raijin|1=Kamo-wake-ikazuchi-no-Kami}}. Chuushichisha. It is located between Tokan-in, the village temple of Enryaku-ji Temple, and Omandokoro.** Ryu Goinsha - Enshrined deity: Goin Hoshi. It is on the left of Ubuya Shrine. ** Nezusha - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}Mikoto. {{ill|lt=Onjoji|Q1756307|qid=Q1756307|1=Mii-dera}} attacked Enryakuji Temple {{jalink|Raigo|Q1166 There is Juhachimei Shrine (Nezumi no Miya) that enshrines {{ill|lt=Tetsu Nezumi|Q10564290|qid=Q10564290|1=Tesso}}), which is the disguised form of 5027}}, and it is said that this shrine is where the rat was sealed away. It is located south of Tokan-in Temple. ** {{ill|lt=Hiyoshi Toshogu Shrine|Q11505052|qid=Q11505052|1=Hiyoshi Tōshō-gū}} - Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, Hiyoshi Okami, {{ill|lt=Matara God|Q6380689|qid=Q6380689|1=Matarajin}}. It is located south of {{ill|lt=Cable Sakamoto Station|Q5015655|qid=Q5015655|1=Cable Sakamoto Station}} of {{ill|lt=Mt. Hiei Railway Line|Q7402803|qid=Q7402803|lt=Mt. Hiei Sakamoto Cable|1=Sakamoto Cable}}. *** Main shrine (Important cultural property) - It was rebuilt in 11th year ({{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|qid=Q1192913|1=Kan'ei}} ({{ill|lt=1634|Q6872|qid=Q6872|1=1634}}) in the Gongen-zukuri style, which connects the main shrine and the worship hall with a stone wall, and is said to have preceded Nikko Toshogu Shrine and became its prototype. *** Stone room (Important cultural property) *** Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) *** Transparent fence (Important cultural property) *** Karamon (Important Cultural Property) **{{ill|lt=Karasaki Shrine|Q11418537|qid=Q11418537|1=Karasaki Shrine}} - Enshrined deity: Onnabetsu Dumyou. Located in Otsu City {{ill|lt=Karasaki (Otsu City)|Q11418535|qid=Q11418535|lt=Karasaki|1=Karasaki}}. The carved roof shows the characteristics of Hiyoshi-zukuri. File:Hiyoshi-taisha higashihongu-haiden01n4592.jpg|Higashihongu Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha higashihongu-romon01nt3200.jpg|Higashihongu Tower Gate (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha07n4592.jpg|From left: Usawakamiya, Kengu Shrine, Kohakusan Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Kitano Shrine, Hakusan Hime Shrine Worship Hall File:Hiyoshi-taisha usagu-haiden01n4592.jpg|Usamiya Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha usagu-honden1.jpg|Usamiya Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha shirayamahime-jinja-haiden01n4592.jpg|Hakusan Hime Shrine worship hall, main hall (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha shirayamahime-jinja-honden01st3200.jpg|Hakusan Hime Shrine Main Hall (Important Cultural Property)File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-haiden01nt3200.jpg|Kishita Shrine Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg|Kishita Shrine Main Hall (Important Cultural Property) File:Sannomiya and Ushio Shrines, Hiyoshi Taisha.jpg|Sannomiya Shrine (left)/Ushio Shrine File:Hiyoshi-taisha ninomiyabashi01s4592.jpg|Ninomiya Bridge (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha omiyabashi02s4592.jpg|Omiya Bridge (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha02n4592.jpg|Hashii Bridge (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha sannomiya-jinja.jpg|Sannomiya Shrine Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha ushio-jinja.jpg|Ushio Shrine Worship Hall (Important Cultural Property) File:Hiyoshi-taisha Koganenooiwa.jpg|Hiyoshi-taisha Koganenooiwa === Cultural assets === {{Attribute source|date=August 2025|section=1}} ;national treasure * Nishihongu Main Hall - Rebuilt in 1586. * Higashi Hongu Main Hall - Rebuilt in 1595. ;Important cultural property The following buildings were built in the {{ill|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|Q319531|qid=Q319531|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} except for Toshogu Shrine (built in the Edo period). * Nishihongu Worship Hall * Nishimotogu Tower Gate * Higashihongu Worship Hall attached: 1 old ceiling coffer * Higashi Hongu Tower Gate * Setsusha Usamiya 2 buildings ** Main shrine ** Worship Hall * Setsusha Jugejinja 2 buildings - Jugejinja is located on the same site as Higashihongu, and has a unique layout in which the approach to Higashihongu and the approach to Jugejinja intersect at right angles. ** Main shrine ** Worship Hall *Setsusha Hakusan Hime Shrine 2 buildings ** Main shrine ** Worship Hall * Setsusha Ushio Shrine 2 buildings - Ushio Shrine and Sannomiya Shrine are located on Mt. Hachioji, and their worship halls are built on hanging cliffs. ** Main shrine ** Worship Hall *Setsusha Sannomiya Shrine 2 buildings ** Main shrine ** Worship Hall * 3 Toshogu shrines (see {{ill|lt=Hiyoshi Toshogu|Q11505052|qid=Q11505052|1=Hiyoshi Tōshō-gū}}) ** Main shrine, stone room, worship hall (1 building) ** Karamon ** Transparent fence * Three Hiyoshi Mihashi bridges - All of them are stone bridges. ** Omiya Bridge ** Hashirii Bridge ** Ninomiya Bridge * 7 Hiyoshi Sanno gilt bronze mikoshi ;Nationally designated historic site * Hiyoshi Shrine precincts ;Shiga Prefecture designated tangible cultural property* Hiyoshi Taisha Torii (Sanno Torii) - Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1940|Q18633|qid=Q18633|1=1940}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}15). ;Otsu City designated tangible cultural property * Main shrine of Omonoki Shrine ; Otsu City designated historic site/scenic spot * Karasaki Shrine precincts ;Otsu City designated intangible folk cultural property * {{ill|lt=Sanno Festival (Otsu City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Sanno Festival}} - Mainly held from April 12th to 15th. ;Cultural properties whose designation has been canceled * Toshogu Bridge (Stone Bridge) - Former national treasure building. {{ill|lt=1935|Q18658|qid=Q18658|1=1935}} (1935) It was washed away by a flood on June 29th, and the designation was cancelled. === Main festivals === [[Image:Hiyoshi Taisha Momiji 1.jpg|thumb|180px|Momiji Festival ({{ill|lt=Light-up|Q17218409|qid=Q17218409|1=Light-up}})]] * Oto Opening Ceremony (January 1st) - This is the Saitan Festival, and before sunrise, under the light of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=torches|1=UNKNOWN}}, Nodayu Katayama performed the {{ill|lt=Noh|Q226887|qid=Q226887|1=Noh}} performance at Nishimoto Shrine. (Noh)|Q11610440|Old Man}}' (Old Man of Hiyoshi), and {{ill|lt=Noh song|Q8062662|qid=Q8062662|1=Yōkyoku}}'{{ill|lt=Takasago (Noh)|Q11672594|qid=Q11672594|lt=Takasago|1=Takasago (play)}}' is dedicated at Higashi Main Shrine. It is also called ignition because there is a custom of taking the flame from the torch home and using it for cooking. * Sanno Festival (April 14) - It is said to have its origins around 1,300 years ago, when Miwa Myojin moved to Sakamoto, and the locals dedicated a large {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakaki|1=UNKNOWN}}. The festival period lasts for one and a half months, including the raising of the mikoshi, the Oosakaki ritual, the horse ritual, the tea ceremony, the flower crossing, the Yoimiya ochi, the Awazu ritual, the return of the mikoshi, the Tori ritual, and the Funaro ritual. In particular, the mikoshi of the Sanno Seven Shrines is said to be magnificent, and contrasts with the silence of the Oosakaki ritual.* Sanno Worship Lecture (May 26th) - It is said that the origin of this tradition is that in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Manju|Q1194155|qid=Q1194155|1=Manju (era)}} ({{ill|lt=1025|Q19267|qid=Q19267|1=1025}}), when it was lamented that monks were only working as {{ill|lt=mono warriors|Q1475695|qid=Q1475695|1=Sōhei}} without training, the Lotus Hakkakko was held at the Nishimoto Shrine to enshrine Hiyoshi Okami. After the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=purification|1=UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=congratulations|Q1760119|qid=Q1760119|1=Norito}}, a question and answer session on the {{ill|lt=Lotus Sutra|Q861212|qid=Q861212|1=Lotus Sutra}} is held, and the Shiga Prefectural Shrine and Shrine Agency calls it ``a valuable festival that conveys to the present the state of the syncretization of Shinto and Buddhism.'' * Mitarashi Festival (July) - A {{ill|lt=Great Purification|Q11261910|qid=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} Shinto ritual held at {{ill|lt=Karasaki Shrine|Q11418537|qid=Q11418537|1=Karasaki Shrine}}, which is a separate shrine. The festival includes a grand purification ceremony, walking through a cogon grass ring, a puppet parade, and the burning of goma wood on {{ill|lt=Lake Biwa|Q200239|qid=Q200239|1=Lake Biwa}}. It is said to have divine virtues for diseases of the lower body and gynecological diseases. Furthermore, Karasaki Shrine is said to be one of the purification sites for Nanase. * {{ill|lt=Autumn leaves|Q1156320|qid=Q1156320|lt=Momiji|1=Autumn leaf color}} Festival (November) - In addition to various events, {{ill|lt=light-up|Q17218409|qid=Q17218409|1=light-up}} is held throughout the Sakamoto area. === Local information === ; Location :* {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|qid=Q131358|1=Shiga Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Otsu City|Q202907|qid=Q202907|1=Ōtsu}}{{ill|lt=Sakamoto (Otsu City)|Q11425171|qid=Q11425171|lt=Sakamoto|1=Sakamoto}}5-1-1 : ; Transportation access :{{underline|Railway}} :* 5 minutes north from {{ill|lt=Mt.
